<code>
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

void solve() {
    int n, q;
    cin >> n >> q;
    vll a(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        cin >> a[i];
    }

    vector<vll> v(n + 1);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        v[a[i]].pb(i);
    }

    mt19937 rng(chrono::steady_clock::now().time_since_epoch().count());
    uniform_real_distribution<double> dist(0.0, 1.0);

    while (q--) {
        int l, r;
        cin >> l >> r;
        --l; --r;
        ll ans = 1;
        set<ll> s;
        for (int j = 0; j < 25; ++j) {
            double rnd = dist(rng);
            int idx = int(rnd * (r - l + 1)) + l;
            ll val = a[idx];
            if (s.count(val)) continue;
            s.insert(val);
            if ((int)v[val].size() <= (r - l + 2) / 2) continue;
            int left = lower_bound(v[val].begin(), v[val].end(), l) - v[val].begin();
            int right = upper_bound(v[val].begin(), v[val].end(), r) - v[val].begin();
            if (right - left > (r - l + 2) / 2) {
                ans = 2 * (right - left) - (r - l + 1);
                break;
            }
        }
        cout << ans << '\n';
    }
}

int main() {
    fastio;
    solve();
    return 0;
}
</code>
reference.think:
Okay so we have an array a of length n, and q queries each asking for a range (l,r). For each range, we need to partition the subarray into the minimum number of pieces (subsequences, not necessarily contiguous) such that in each piece of length x, no value appears more than ceil(x/2) times. The constraints: n,q ≤ 3×10^5, time limit 3s, memory 512MB.

The greedy online approach: for each query, scan from l to r left to right. Maintain a current piece as we go — keep a frequency map for the current piece and its total length. For each new element a[i], check if adding it would make any value's count exceed ceil((len+1)/2). If it would, finalize the current piece (increment piece count), start a new piece with just this element, reset the frequency map. Otherwise, add it to the current piece and update the map.

Let me trace Example 1. Array: [1, 3, 2, 3, 3, 2]. Queries: (1,6) and (2,5).

Query 1: l=1, r=6, subarray = [1,3,2,3,3,2].
Start piece 1: empty, len=0, freq={}.
i=1: a[1]=1. len+1=1, ceil(1/2)=1. freq[1] would be 1, not >1. Add. piece=[1], len=1, freq={1:1}.
i=2: a[2]=3. len+1=2, ceil(2/2)=1. freq[3] would be 1, not >1. Add. piece=[1,3], len=2, freq={1:1, 3:1}.
i=3: a[3]=2. len+1=3, ceil(3/2)=2. freq[2] would be 1, not >2. Add. piece=[1,3,2], len=3, freq={1:1,3:1,2:1}.
i=4: a[4]=3. len+1=4, ceil(4/2)=2. freq[3] would become 2, not >2. Add. piece=[1,3,2,3], len=4, freq={1:1,3:2,2:1}.
i=5: a[5]=3. len+1=5, ceil(5/2)=3. freq[3] would become 3, not >3. Add. piece=[1,3,2,3,3], len=5, freq={1:1,3:3,2:1}.
i=6: a[6]=2. len+1=6, ceil(6/2)=3. freq[2] would become 2, not >3. Add. piece=[1,3,2,3,3,2], len=6, freq={1:1,3:3,2:2}.
Finished. Only 1 piece. Output 1. Correct — example output is 1.

Query 2: l=2, r=5, subarray = [3,2,3,3].
Start piece 1: empty, len=0, freq={}.
i=2: a[2]=3. len+1=1, ceil(1/2)=1. freq[3]=1, not >1. Add. piece=[3], len=1, freq={3:1}.
i=3: a[3]=2. len+1=2, ceil(2/2)=1. freq[2]=1, not >1. Add. piece=[3,2], len=2, freq={3:1,2:1}.
i=4: a[4]=3. len+1=3, ceil(3/2)=2. freq[3] would become 2, not >2. Add. piece=[3,2,3], len=3, freq={3:2,2:1}.
i=5: a[5]=3. len+1=4, ceil(4/2)=2. freq[3] would become 3, and 3 > 2. Violation! Finalize piece 1. Start piece 2 with a[5]=3. piece count = 2.
Finished. Output 2. Correct — example output is 2.

So the greedy works on the example. But does it always give the minimum number of pieces? Let me think about a case where the greedy might make a suboptimal choice by finalizing a piece too early or too late.

Consider a range where the majority element appears many times. Suppose the subarray is [1,1,1,2,2]. The greedy:
i=1: a[1]=1. len=1, ceil=1, freq[1]=1. Add.
i=2: a[2]=1. len=2, ceil=1, freq[1]=2 >1. Violation! Finalize piece 1. Start piece 2 with a[2]=1. count=2.
i=3: a[3]=1. piece 2: len=1, freq={1:1}. len+1=2, ceil=1, freq[1] would be 2 >1. Violation! Finalize piece 2. Start piece 3 with a[3]=1. count=3.
i=4: a[4]=2. piece 3: len=1, freq={1:1}. len+1=2, ceil=1, freq[2] would be 1, not >1. Add. piece 3=[1,2], len=2, freq={1:1,2:1}.
i=5: a[5]=2. piece 3: len=2, freq={1:1,2:1}. len+1=3, ceil=2, freq[2] would be 2, not >2. Add. piece 3=[1,2,2], len=3, freq={1:1,2:2}.
Total pieces: 3.

But is 3 minimal? Let me see if I can do better. The subarray has three 1's and two 2's. In any piece of length x, a value can appear at most ceil(x/2) times. If I put all three 1's in one piece, that piece would need length at least 5 (since ceil(5/2)=3, so 3 ≤ 3). But the whole subarray is length 5, so one piece of all 5 elements: 1 appears 3 times, ceil(5/2)=3, 3 ≤ 3 — valid! So the answer should be 1, not 3. The greedy gave 3 because it locked the first 1 into a piece alone, then the second 1 into another piece alone, before it ever saw the 2's that could have diluted the concentration of 1's.

oh wait — greedy finalized piece 1 at i=2 because freq[1]=2 > ceil(2/2)=1, but it could have kept going and added 2's later to bring the ratio down.

LEARNING: Greedy prematurely splits when majority element appears consecutively — [1,1,1,2,2] needs 1 piece but greedy produces 3 by splitting at the second 1 before seeing diluting elements.

I'll binary search on the answer k — the minimum number of pieces. For a fixed k, I need to check whether the range [l, r] can be partitioned into at most k beautiful subsequences. The beauty condition: in a piece of length x, no value appears more than ceil(x/2) times. That means the majority element (if any) can appear at most ceil(x/2) times. If a piece has no element exceeding half its length, it's valid.

For the feasibility check with k pieces, I can think of it as a flow problem. Each element in the range is a unit of flow that must be assigned to one of k pieces. Each piece has a capacity constraint: for any value v, the number of copies of v assigned to that piece cannot exceed ceil(size_of_piece / 2). But the piece sizes aren't fixed — they're determined by how many elements we assign to each piece. This is circular: the capacity depends on the total assignment.

Let me rephrase: given k pieces, I need to assign the multiset of elements in [l, r] to k subsequences such that in each subsequence, no value is a strict majority. Equivalently, for each piece, the most frequent element appears at most half the piece's length. This is a classic scheduling / load-balancing constraint.

Let me think about the structure. In the whole range, let f be the frequency of the most frequent element. If f ≤ ceil((r-l+1)/2), then the whole range is already beautiful as one piece — answer is 1. The problem becomes interesting when there's a majority element that appears more than half the time. Let's call that element M, and its count in the range is f. The remaining elements (non-M) total len - f, where len = r-l+1.

When f > ceil(len/2), we can't put everything in one piece. We need to split. The key observation: each piece can absorb at most (size_of_piece - f_in_piece) non-M elements to dilute M, but M's count in a piece must be ≤ ceil(size/2). If a piece has m copies of M and t copies of other elements, we need m ≤ ceil((m+t)/2), which simplifies to m ≤ t+1 (since ceil((m+t)/2) ≥ (m+t)/2, so 2m ≤ m+t+1 → m ≤ t+1). Actually let me do it carefully: m ≤ ceil((m+t)/2). If m+t is even, ceil((m+t)/2) = (m+t)/2, so m ≤ (m+t)/2 → 2m ≤ m+t → m ≤ t. If m+t is odd, ceil((m+t)/2) = (m+t+1)/2, so m ≤ (m+t+1)/2 → 2m ≤ m+t+1 → m ≤ t+1. So the rule is: m ≤ t+1 always, and m ≤ t when the total length is even. In the worst case (odd length), a piece can have at most one more M than non-M.

So each piece can "neutralize" at most one extra M beyond the non-M elements it contains. If we have f copies of M and s = len - f copies of other elements, then across k pieces, the total number of M copies we can accommodate is at most s + k (since each piece gives us at most +1 slack for M beyond the non-M it contains). Actually, if we distribute the s non-M elements across the k pieces, and each piece i gets t_i non-M elements, then piece i can hold at most t_i + 1 copies of M. Summing over i: total M capacity = sum(t_i + 1) = s + k. So we can accommodate at most s + k copies of M. We have f copies of M. So we need f ≤ s + k, i.e., k ≥ f - s.

But s = len - f, so f - s = f - (len - f) = 2f - len. So k ≥ 2f - len. Since k must be at least 1, the answer would be max(1, 2f - len). Let me test this on Example 1.

Example 1: a = [1, 3, 2, 3, 3, 2], n=6, q=2.
Query 1: l=1, r=6. Range = [1,3,2,3,3,2]. len=6. Frequencies: 1:1, 2:2, 3:3. f=3 (value 3). s=3. 2f - len = 6-6=0. max(1,0)=1. Output says 1. Matches.

Query 2: l=2, r=5. Range = [3,2,3,3]. len=4. Frequencies: 3:3, 2:1. f=3. s=1. 2f - len = 6-4=2. max(1,2)=2. Output says 2. Matches.

So the formula max(1, 2f - len) works for these examples. But wait — this isn't a binary search with flow. The hypothesis says "Binary search on answer with flow-based feasibility check." That's a different approach. Let me actually test the flow-based binary search approach to see if it's needed or if the simple formula is always correct.

Let me try a case where the simple formula might fail. Suppose range = [1,1,1,2,3,4]. len=6. f=3 (value 1). s=3. 2f-len=0 → answer 1. Can we put all in one piece? The piece would be [1,1,1,2,3,4]. Length 6, 1 appears 3 times, ceil(6/2)=3. 3 ≤ 3, valid. Yes.

What about [1,1,1,1,2,3]? len=6, f=4, s=2. 2f-len=8-6=2. Answer 2. Can we do it in 2 pieces? Total M capacity = s + k = 2+2=4. We have 4 copies of 1. Need to assign them. Piece 1: maybe [1,1,2] — length 3, 1 appears 2 times, ceil(3/2)=2, ok. Piece 2: [1,1,3] — same. Works.

What about [1,1,1,1,1,2]? len=6, f=5, s=1. 2f-len=10-6=4. Answer 4. Capacity: s+k=1+4=5. Can we do 4 pieces? We have 5 ones and 1 two. Pieces: [1,2] (len 2, 1≤1 ok), [1] (len 1, 1≤1 ok), [1] (len 1, ok), [1] (len 1, ok), [1] — wait that's 5 pieces. Actually we need 4 pieces. Let's try: Piece 1: [1,2] (1 copy of 1, 1 copy of 2). Piece 2: [1,1] — length 2, 1 appears 2 times, ceil(2/2)=1, 2 > 1, INVALID. So [1,1] alone is invalid. We can't put two 1's together without a non-1 to dilute. With s=1 (only one non-1 element), each piece can have at most one more 1 than non-1. So pieces with 0 non-1 elements can have at most 1 copy of 1. Pieces with the single non-1 element can have at most 2 copies of 1. So max 1's we can place in k pieces: one piece gets the non-1 element and can hold 2 ones, the other k-1 pieces can hold 1 one each. Total = 2 + (k-1)*1 = k+1. We have f=5 ones. Need k+1 ≥ 5 → k ≥ 4. So k=4 works: Piece 1: [1,1,2] (2 ones), Piece 2: [1], Piece 3: [1], Piece 4: [1]. That's 4 pieces, each valid. So answer 4 matches formula.

Now, is the formula always correct? Let me think about a potential counterexample. The derivation assumed we can freely distribute the s non-M elements to maximize M capacity, and the bound f ≤ s + k is both necessary and sufficient. Necessity is clear: each piece i with t_i non-M elements can hold at most t_i + 1 copies of M, sum gives s + k. Sufficiency: can we always achieve this bound? We need to construct k pieces where the total M capacity is exactly used. This is like: we have s non-M elements and f M elements. We want to partition into k subsequences such that in each, M count ≤ non-M count + 1. This is equivalent to: we can always do it as long as f ≤ s + k. The construction: put as many M's as possible with the non-M elements. Specifically, pair each non-M element with an M element (or multiple M's up to the limit). The extreme case is when we have many M's and few non-M's. We can put up to 2 M's with one non-M (like [M, non-M, M] — length 3, M=2, ceil(3/2)=2, valid). Actually [M, non-M, M] has 2 M's and 1 non-M, M ≤ non-M+1 holds (2 ≤ 2). So each non-M can "support" up to 2 M's in a piece. If we have s non-M elements, we can support up to 2s M's in pieces that contain at least one non-M. The remaining M's must go into pieces with no non-M elements, which can hold at most 1 M each. So total M capacity = 2s + (k - s) = s + k, assuming we use all k pieces and s ≤ k (if s > k, we can put multiple non-M's in some pieces, but the formula s + k still holds as an upper bound). Wait, if s > k, then we have more non-M elements than pieces. Then each piece gets at least one non-M element. The capacity per piece with t_i non-M elements is t_i + 1. Sum = s + k. So the formula f ≤ s + k is always the condition. And we can achieve it by a greedy construction: sort the range, take the M elements and interleave them with non-M elements as much as possible, then put leftover M's as singletons. This is essentially the same as the "rearrange to avoid adjacent duplicates" problem but with subsequences instead of contiguous pieces. Since subsequences don't require contiguity, we have full freedom to assign elements to pieces. So the condition f ≤ s + k is both necessary and sufficient.

Therefore, the minimum k is max(1, 2f - len). No binary search or flow needed. The hypothesis suggested binary search with flow, but the problem reduces to a simple formula after analyzing the majority element constraint.

Now, how to answer queries efficiently? For each query (l, r), we need f = frequency of the majority element in that range. n, q ≤ 3×10^5. We need to answer range majority frequency queries quickly. This is a classic problem: range mode query. With n=3×10^5 and q=3×10^5, we need something faster than O(n) per query. One approach: for each value, store its positions in a sorted list. To find the frequency of a value in [l, r], we can binary search. But we don't know which value is the majority element. If we pick a random element from the range, it's the majority with probability > 1/2 if a majority exists. We can use a segment tree with Boyer-Moore majority vote merging, or a randomized approach, or Mo's algorithm. But the time limit is 3s, which is generous. Mo's algorithm with O((n+q)√n) might pass if implemented efficiently. Or we can use a segment tree that stores the majority candidate and count, then verify with binary search on positions. That would be O(log n) per query for the candidate plus O(log n) to verify, total O((n+q) log n). That's well within 3×10^5 log 3×10^5 ≈ 5×10^6 operations.

But wait — the hypothesis I'm testing is specifically "Binary search on answer with flow-based feasibility check." That approach would binary search k from 1 to len, and for each mid, run a flow check. The flow check would need to determine if f ≤ s + mid, which is O(1) after computing f and s. So the binary search would take O(log len) per query, and computing f takes some time. But the formula gives the answer directly without binary search: k = max(1, 2f - len). So the binary search is unnecessary — it's just a wrapper around the same check. The real work is computing f, the majority frequency.

Now, does the flow-based feasibility check add anything? The check "f ≤ s + k" is derived from the capacity argument, which is essentially a flow / matching argument: we're matching M elements to non-M slots. But implementing a full max-flow per query would be far too slow. The hypothesis as stated — "Binary search on answer with flow-based feasibility check" — would mean for each query, for each candidate k, we build a flow network and run max flow. That's completely infeasible: q=3×10^5, binary search adds log(3×10^5) ≈ 19 iterations, each flow network has O(len) nodes and edges, max flow would be O(V E) or worse. Even for a single query, building a flow network on a range of size up to 3×10^5 and running max flow 19 times would be millions of operations per query, times 3×10^5 queries — impossible.

So the hypothesis as literally stated is absurdly slow. But maybe the hypothesis meant "use the capacity condition as the feasibility check" — which is O(1) after finding f. In that case, the binary search is still redundant because the answer is a direct formula.

Let me trace the binary search approach on Example 1, Query 2: l=2, r=5, range=[3,2,3,3], len=4. f=3, s=1. Binary search k from 1 to 4:
  k=2: check if feasible. f=3, s=1. Condition: f ≤ s + k → 3 ≤ 1+2=3. Feasible. So try smaller k.
  k=1: check: 3 ≤ 1+1=2. Not feasible. So answer is 2.
This works but the binary search is just finding the smallest k satisfying 3 ≤ 1+k, which is k ≥ 2. That's just solving k ≥ f - s = 2. Binary search is overkill.

So the hypothesis "Binary search on answer with flow-based feasibility check" is either:
1. A misunderstanding that a full flow network is needed (way too slow), or
2. A redundant binary search on top of the O(1) capacity check (unnecessary but not wrong).

In either case, the core insight is the capacity bound f ≤ s + k, which gives the direct formula. The hypothesis doesn't identify the actual bottleneck (computing range majority frequency), nor does it recognize that the answer simplifies.

wait — the hypothesis proposes binary search + flow, but the problem reduces to a direct formula after a simple capacity argument. The flow check per iteration would be O(1) if we use the capacity bound, making binary search O(log n) per query, but the formula gives O(1) after finding f. The real challenge is finding f efficiently, which the hypothesis doesn't address.

LEARNING: Binary search on k with flow feasibility is unnecessary — the capacity bound f ≤ s + k simplifies to k = max(1, 2f - len), a direct formula. The hypothesis misses that the feasibility check is O(1) and binary search adds no value.

So none of those panned out. The greedy kept splitting prematurely because it couldn't see the whole range to dilute the majority, and binary search on the answer was overkill — the formula for the minimum pieces seemed to be a direct function of the frequency of the most frequent element.

hmm — wait. That formula. If I let len = r-l+1, and let f be the frequency of the most frequent element in the range, then the condition for a single beautiful piece is f ≤ ceil(len/2). If f exceeds that, I need more pieces. But how many? Each piece can absorb at most ceil(piece_len/2) copies of that element. To minimize pieces, I'd want to pack as many copies as possible into each piece. The worst-case is when all f copies are placed into separate pieces? No, that's too pessimistic. I can pair each copy of the majority with a different element to dilute it.

Let me think: In a piece of length x, I can put at most ceil(x/2) copies of the majority. So the total capacity across k pieces, if I could choose lengths arbitrarily, is sum ceil(x_i/2). But the total length is len. The maximum total majority capacity is ceil(len/2) if all pieces are size 1? Wait, if every piece is size 1, ceil(1/2)=1, so total capacity = k. But len = k in that case, so capacity = len. That's larger than ceil(len/2) for len>1. So small pieces give more capacity per total length? That seems backwards. Let's derive properly.

Suppose I have k pieces. Let their lengths be L1,...,Lk, sum = len. The total number of majority copies I can place is sum ceil(Li/2). I want to minimize k such that this sum ≥ f. What's the maximum of sum ceil(Li/2) for fixed len and k? To maximize capacity, I should make pieces as small as possible, because ceil(1/2)=1 gives a ratio of 1, while for larger pieces the ratio approaches 1/2. Actually, ceil(2/2)=1 gives ratio 1/2, ceil(3/2)=2 gives ratio 2/3. So the ratio is highest for size 1 pieces (ratio 1), then size 3 (2/3), size 2 (1/2), size 4 (2/4=1/2). So to maximize total capacity, I should use as many size 1 and size 3 pieces as possible.

But I also need to fit the total length len. Let me try an extreme: if f is just over ceil(len/2), maybe k=2 is enough. Let's test on Example 1, query (2,5): a=[3,2,3,3], len=4. The most frequent element is 3, f=3. ceil(4/2)=2, so f=3 > 2, need more than 1 piece. Can I do with 2 pieces? Suppose two pieces of lengths 2 and 2. Each can hold ceil(2/2)=1 copy of 3. Total capacity = 2, but I have 3 copies, so no. Lengths 3 and 1: capacities ceil(3/2)=2 and ceil(1/2)=1, total=3. That works! So k=2 is possible. The formula? The extra copies beyond the single-piece limit: f - ceil(len/2) = 3 - 2 = 1. And k = 1 + (extra copies)? That gives 2. Let's check query (1,6): len=6, a=[1,3,2,3,3,2]. Most frequent is 3, f=3? Wait, count: 1 appears once, 3 appears three times, 2 appears twice. So f=3. ceil(6/2)=3, so f ≤ ceil(len/2) → k=1. The formula extra = f - ceil(len/2) = 0, k=1. That matches.

So the minimum pieces k = max(1, 2*f - len)? Let's test on (2,5): 2*f - len = 2*3 - 4 = 2, max(1,2)=2. On (1,6): 2*3 - 6 = 0, max(1,0)=1. That works. But why? Let's derive: We need sum ceil(Li/2) ≥ f. Minimize k. The maximum total capacity for given len and k is achieved by making as many pieces as possible of length 1 or 3. Actually, a simpler bound: For any piece, ceil(Li/2) ≥ Li/2. So total capacity ≥ len/2. That's not tight. Better: ceil(Li/2) = (Li+1)//2. So total capacity = sum (Li+1)//2 = (len + k)//2? Because sum (Li+1)//2 ≥ (sum Li + k)/2? Actually, (Li+1)//2 = floor((Li+1)/2). For integers, floor((Li+1)/2) = (Li+1)//2. Sum over i gives (len + k - t)/2 where t is the number of even Li? Hmm messy.

Let's think differently: In the optimal packing, each copy of the majority that exceeds the single-piece threshold must be placed in a separate piece with a "diluter". Actually, the excess beyond ceil(len/2) is exactly the number of extra pieces needed. Because in a single piece, the majority can occupy at most ceil(len/2) spots. Any additional copies must go into new pieces where they become the majority again, but each new piece can take at most ceil(len_i/2) copies. The most efficient way is to pair each extra copy with one non-majority element in a piece of length 2, giving capacity 1 per piece. So each extra copy requires one new piece of length 2. But the total length is fixed, so adding a piece of length 2 reduces the length available for the main piece. This is getting circular.

Wait — I recall a known result: the answer is max(1, 2*f - len). Let me verify with another case: len=5, f=4. 2*f - len = 3, so k=3. Can I do with 3 pieces? Pieces lengths 2,2,1: capacities 1,1,1 total=3 < 4. Lengths 3,1,1: capacities 2,1,1 total=4. Yes, works. So the formula seems plausible.

Now the core problem: for each query (l,r), I need f, the frequency of the most frequent element in that subarray. And n,q up to 3e5 — I need O(log n) per query. There's a classic trick: if an element occurs more than half the length, it's the majority. But here f might be less than half. Still, only one element can have f > ceil(len/2). So I can find a candidate for the majority via a segment tree that stores the majority candidate and its count? The Boyer-Moore majority vote algorithm can be made persistent. Actually, I can build a segment tree where each node stores a candidate and a net count, merging by: if left.cand == right.cand, sum counts; else subtract smaller count from larger, keep the winner. That gives a candidate that is the majority if a majority exists, otherwise it's some element. Then I need to verify its actual frequency in the range. If it's not actually > ceil(len/2), then f ≤ ceil(len/2) and answer=1. If it is, then f is its frequency, and answer = 2*f - len.

But how to get the actual frequency of that candidate in O(log n)? I can preprocess for each value a list of positions where it occurs, and for a given range, count = upper_bound(r) - lower_bound(l). That's O(log n) per query with binary search on the list. So total O(q log n).

Let me trace on Example 1. Preprocess lists: pos[1]=[1], pos[2]=[3,6], pos[3]=[2,4,5]. Query (1,6): len=6. Segment tree query gives candidate? Let's simulate merging manually: elements [1,3,2,3,3,2]. Boyer-Moore on whole array: cand=3, count=1? Actually run: start cand=1, cnt=1. Next 3: different, cnt-- to 0, set cand=3, cnt=1. Next 2: different, cnt-- to 0, set cand=2, cnt=1. Next 3: different, cnt-- to 0, set cand=3, cnt=1. Next 3: same, cnt++ to 2. Next 2: different, cnt-- to 1. Final cand=3. So candidate is 3. Now check frequency of 3 in [1,6]: positions [2,4,5] → count=3. ceil(len/2)=3, so f=3 ≤ 3 → answer=1. Correct.

Query (2,5): subarray [3,2,3,3], len=4. Boyer-Moore: start cand=3, cnt=1. Next 2: diff, cnt-- to 0, set cand=2, cnt=1. Next 3: diff, cnt-- to 0, set cand=3, cnt=1. Next 3: same, cnt++ to 2. Final cand=3. Frequency of 3 in [2,5]: positions [2,4,5] within range? 2 is in, 4 in, 5 in → count=3. ceil(4/2)=2, f=3 > 2. So answer = 2*f - len = 6-4=2. Correct.

But what if the candidate from Boyer-Moore is not the actual most frequent? Suppose no element has f > ceil(len/2). Then the candidate might be some arbitrary element. Its actual frequency will be ≤ ceil(len/2), so the condition f > ceil(len/2) will fail, and answer=1. That's fine because the true maximum frequency also ≤ ceil(len/2), so answer=1 anyway. The formula 2*f - len would give ≤1? Actually if f ≤ ceil(len/2), then 2*f - len ≤ 2*ceil(len/2) - len ≤ 1? For len even, ceil(len/2)=len/2, so 2*f - len ≤ 0. For len odd, ceil(len/2)=(len+1)/2, so 2*f - len ≤ 1. So max(1, 2*f - len) would still give 1. So using the candidate's frequency works even if it's not the global maximum? Wait, but if the true most frequent element has higher frequency than the candidate, then using the candidate's frequency might underestimate the answer. Example: len=5, elements [1,1,2,2,3]. True f=2 (for 1 and 2). ceil(5/2)=3, so f=2 ≤3 → answer=1. Candidate from Boyer-Moore? Let's see: start 1 cnt=1, next 1 cnt=2, next 2 diff cnt-- to1, next 2 diff cnt-- to0 set cand=2 cnt=1, next 3 diff cnt-- to0 set cand=3 cnt=1. Candidate=3, frequency=1. Using f=1 gives answer=1. So still correct. But is there a case where the true most frequent element has f > ceil(len/2) but the candidate is a different element with lower frequency? That's impossible — if an element has f > ceil(len/2), it is the majority, and Boyer-Moore will find it. So the candidate's frequency is exactly the maximum frequency if a majority exists, otherwise it's some element with frequency ≤ ceil(len/2). Perfect.

So algorithm: preprocess position lists for each value. Build a segment tree that stores (candidate, count) for merging via Boyer-Moore. For each query (l,r):
  len = r-l+1.
  (cand, _) = segtree.query(l,r).
  f = count of cand in [l,r] using binary search on pos[cand].
  if f > ceil(len/2): answer = 2*f - len.
  else: answer = 1.

Let's trace edge case: len=3, elements [1,1,2]. True f=2, ceil(3/2)=2, so f=2 not strictly greater? Condition is "strictly more than ceil(x/2)" for a piece. Here f=2, ceil(3/2)=2, so not strictly more → beautiful piece possible, answer=1. Our condition: f > ceil(len/2)? 2 > 2? false, so answer=1. Good.

Another: len=2, elements [1,1]. f=2, ceil(2/2)=1, f > 1 → answer = 2*f - len = 4-2=2. Indeed, need two pieces of size 1 each.

Implementation details: segment tree merge: node with (cand, cnt). Merge left and right: if left.cand == right.cand, new cand = left.cand, new cnt = left.cnt + right.cnt. Else: if left.cnt > right.cnt, new cand = left.cand, new cnt = left.cnt - right.cnt. Else new cand = right.cand, new cnt = right.cnt - left.cnt. This is the Boyer-Moore merge.

Time: O((n+q) log n). Memory: O(n) for pos lists, O(n) for segtree.

aha! — so the key was realizing that the problem reduces to finding the frequency of the potential majority element, and that the minimum pieces formula is a simple linear function of that frequency and the length, not requiring any search or flow. The greedy failed because it tried to build pieces online without global frequency knowledge; the binary search was redundant because the answer is directly computable once you have f. The segment tree with Boyer-Moore merging elegantly finds the candidate in O(log n), and the position lists give the exact count.

finetuned.full: To solve this problem, we need to determine the minimum number of subsequences needed for a given range [l, r] such that no subsequence contains a value occurring more than ceil(x/2) times, where x is the length of the subsequence. The brute-force approach would be too slow because we'd need to explore all possible partitions for each query, which is exponential. We'll focus on using a sliding window to find valid partitions efficiently. Here’s the plan: 1. **Sliding Window**: For each query [l, r], maintain a sliding window over the array from index l to r. 2. **Count Occurrences**: Track how many times each element appears within the current window. 3. **Partition at Invalid Points**: Whenever an element exceeds the allowed frequency, split the window there. 4. **Optimization**: Instead of splitting immediately, keep extending the window as long as possible while staying valid, then split once it becomes invalid. This reduces the number of splits. Let's walk through the logic with the example provided: **Example 1:** - Array: `1 3 2 3 3 2` - Query: `[1, 6]` Initial window: `[1, 3, 2, 3, 3, 2]` Occurrences: `{1:1, 3:3, 2:2}` Max allowed occurrence: `ceil(6/2)=3`, but 3 occurs 3 times, which is exactly allowed. So no split needed. Result = 1. **Example 2:** - Array: `1 3 2 3 3 2` - Query: `[2, 5]` Window: `[3, 2, 3, 3]` Occurrences: `{3:3, 2:1}` Allowed: `ceil(4/2)=2`. 3 occurs 3 times, exceeding allowed. Split at first occurrence of 3: `[3]` and `[2, 3, 3]`. Result = 2. Now let's implement this logic in C++. We'll use a map to track occurrences and a set to store indices where splits should happen. #include using namespace std; int n, q; vector a; // Function to count elements within a range [l, r] and ensure beauty condition int min_pieces(int l, int r) { vector freq(n+1, 0); set splits; int max_len = r - l + 1; int allowed = (max_len + 1) / 2; for (int i = l; i <= r; ++i) { int val = a[i]; freq[val]++; if (freq[val] > allowed) { splits.insert(i); } } // Add a sentinel at the end to handle the last segment splits.insert(r+1); return splits.size(); } void solve() { cin >> n >> q; a.resize(n); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { cin >> a[i]; } while (q--) { int l, r; cin >> l >> r; cout << min_pieces(l-1, r-1) << '\n'; } } int main() { ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); solve(); return 0; }

To solve this problem, we need to determine the minimum number of subsequences needed for a given range [l, r] such that no subsequence contains a value occurring more than ceil(x/2) times, where x is the length of the subsequence. The brute-force approach would be too slow because we'd need to explore all possible partitions for each query, which is exponential. 

We'll focus on using a sliding window to find valid partitions efficiently. Here’s the plan:

1. **Sliding Window**: For each query [l, r], maintain a sliding window over the array from index l to r.
2. **Count Occurrences**: Track how many times each element appears within the current window.
3. **Partition at Invalid Points**: Whenever an element exceeds the allowed frequency, split the window there.
4. **Optimization**: Instead of splitting immediately, keep extending the window as long as possible while staying valid, then split once it becomes invalid. This reduces the number of splits.

Let's walk through the logic with the example provided:

**Example 1:**
- Array: `1 3 2 3 3 2`
- Query: `[1, 6]`

Initial window: `[1, 3, 2, 3, 3, 2]`  
Occurrences: `{1:1, 3:3, 2:2}`  
Max allowed occurrence: `ceil(6/2)=3`, but 3 occurs 3 times, which is exactly allowed. So no split needed. Result = 1.

**Example 2:**
- Array: `1 3 2 3 3 2`
- Query: `[2, 5]`

Window: `[3, 2, 3, 3]`  
Occurrences: `{3:3, 2:1}`  
Allowed: `ceil(4/2)=2`. 3 occurs 3 times, exceeding allowed. Split at first occurrence of 3: `[3]` and `[2, 3, 3]`. Result = 2.

Now let's implement this logic in C++. We'll use a map to track occurrences and a set to store indices where splits should happen.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
A subarray is a continuous part of array.

Yarik recently found an array $$$a$$$ of $$$n$$$ elements and became very interested in finding the maximum sum of a non empty subarray. However, Yarik doesn't like consecutive integers with the same parity, so the subarray he chooses must have alternating parities for adjacent elements.

For example, $$$[1, 2, 3]$$$ is acceptable, but $$$[1, 2, 4]$$$ is not, as $$$2$$$ and $$$4$$$ are both even and adjacent.

You need to help Yarik by finding the maximum sum of such a subarray.

Time limit: 1s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
The first line contains an integer $$$t$$$ $$$(1 \le t \le 10^4)$$$ — number of test cases. Each test case is described as follows.

The first line of each test case contains an integer $$$n$$$ $$$(1 \le n \le 2 \cdot 10^5)$$$ — length of the array.

The second line of each test case contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$$$ $$$(-10^3 \le a_i \le 10^3)$$$ — elements of the array.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ for all test cases does not exceed $$$2 \cdot 10^5$$$.

Output:
For each test case, output a single integer — the answer to the problem.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    7
    5
    1 2 3 4 5
    4
    9 9 8 8
    6
    -1 4 -1 0 5 -4
    4
    -1 2 4 -3
    1
    -1000
    3
    101 -99 101
    20
    -10 5 -8 10 6 -10 7 9 -2 -6 7 2 -4 6 -1 7 -6 -7 4 1
    Output:
    15
    17
    8
    4
    -1000
    101
    10

reference.full: Alright, let's start. We have t up to 10^4, total n across all tests ≤ 2×10^5, so per test we need O(n) or O(n log n). First thought: This is like maximum subarray sum (Kadane's algorithm) but with a constraint: adjacent elements in the chosen subarray must have different parity (odd/even). That means within the subarray, parity must alternate. If we think about splitting the array into segments where consecutive elements have alternating parity — but wait, the subarray must be contiguous in the original array, so we can't skip elements. Let’s test on the first example: [1,2,3,4,5] — all consecutive pairs have alternating parity? 1(odd) → 2(even) ok, 2(even) → 3(odd) ok, 3(odd) → 4(even) ok, 4(even) → 5(odd) ok. So the whole array satisfies the condition. Max sum is sum of all = 15. Second example: [9,9,8,8] 9(odd) → 9(odd) not ok (same parity). So longest alternating parity subarrays: [9] sum 9, [9,8] sum 17, [8] sum 8, [8] sum 8. Max is 17. So basically, we can only take contiguous segments where each adjacent pair has different parity. Once two consecutive elements have same parity, the subarray must break there. So the original array can be split into maximal alternating-parity segments. Within each such segment, we want max subarray sum (normal Kadane). But wait — even inside a maximal alternating-parity segment, Kadane can drop negative prefixes, but here dropping a prefix might break the parity alternation? Actually, if we take a subarray inside a maximal alternating-parity segment, it will still have alternating parity because the whole segment already alternates, so any contiguous part of it also alternates. So yes, inside each maximal alternating-parity segment, we can run Kadane to get max subarray sum, then take max over all segments. Let’s test on third example: [-1,4,-1,0,5,-4] Check parity alternation: -1(odd) → 4(even) ok, 4(even) → -1(odd) ok, -1(odd) → 0(even) ok, 0(even) → 5(odd) ok, 5(odd) → -4(even) ok. So whole array is one maximal alternating-parity segment. Run Kadane: Start at -1, sum=-1, better to restart at 4, sum=4, add -1 → 3, add 0 → 3, add 5 → 8, add -4 → 4. Max seen = 8. Matches example output. Fourth example: [-1,2,4,-3] -1(odd) → 2(even) ok, 2(even) → 4(even) not ok → break. So segments: [-1,2] and [4,-3]. First segment Kadane: -1→2 sum=2, max=2. Second segment: 4 sum=4, 4+(-3)=1, max=4. Overall max=4, matches output. So approach: Iterate through array, track current alternating-parity segment, run Kadane inside it, reset Kadane when parity alternation fails. But careful: When parity alternation fails, the new segment starts at the second of the two same-parity elements? Actually, if a[i] and a[i+1] have same parity, subarray cannot include both, so maximal alternating-parity segments are: start at some index, go until before the violation. If violation at i and i+1, segment ends at i, new segment starts at i+1. So we just start new Kadane at i+1. We need to handle single element segments: if only one element, Kadane just takes it. Let’s test tricky case: [101, -99, 101] 101(odd) → -99(odd) not ok → break. Segments: [101], [-99], [101]. Max is 101. Works. Edge: all negative numbers? Example: [-1000] → max = -1000. Complexity: O(n) total. Wait — is this always correct? Let’s check the last example quickly with a mental simulation: Array length 20, alternating parity mostly but maybe some same-parity adjacent. Our method: scan, keep current sum, when parity same, reset. But reset means start new sum at current element. Let’s test a short case: [5, -10, 3] all odd/even? 5(odd) → -10(even) ok, -10(even) → 3(odd) ok, so one segment. Kadane: 5 sum=5, 5+(-10)=-5, restart at -10? no, better to keep? Wait, Kadane: if current sum < 0, reset. But here 5 + (-10) = -5 < 0, so reset at next 3. sum=3. Max=5. Works. But careful: parity constraint means we cannot take subarray across a same-parity adjacent pair. So when we see same parity, we must reset current sum to current element (start new segment). That’s already handled. So final plan: Initialize max_sum = a[0], curr_sum = a[0]. For i from 1 to n-1: If a[i] % 2 == a[i-1] % 2 (same parity), reset curr_sum = a[i]. Else, curr_sum = max(a[i], curr_sum + a[i]) (Kadane step). Update max_sum = max(max_sum, curr_sum). That’s O(n) per test, total O(total n). Check example 2: [9,9,8,8] i=1: 9 same parity as 9 → curr_sum=9, max_sum=9. i=2: 8 different parity from 9 → curr_sum=max(8, 9+8=17)=17, max_sum=17. i=3: 8 same parity as 8 → curr_sum=8, max_sum=17. Works. Seems correct. #include using namespace std; #define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); #define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end() #define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend() #define sz(x) (int)(x).size() #define ll long long #define pb push_back #define pob pop_back #define vll vector #define pll pair #define vpll vector #define endl '\n' #define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a)) #define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a)) ll gcd(ll a, ll b) { while (a > 0) { ll temp = b % a; b = a; a = temp; } return b; } ll lcm(ll a, ll b) { return a / gcd(a, b) * b; } ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) { ll t = n / k; if (n % k == 0) return t; return t + 1; } ll pw(ll a, ll b) { ll r = 1; while (b) { if (b & 1) r *= a; a *= a; b >>= 1; } return r; } ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) { ll r = 1 % mod; a %= mod; while (b) { if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod; a = a * a % mod; b >>= 1; } return r; } void solve() { ll n; cin >> n; vll a(n); for (ll i = 0; i < n; ++i) cin >> a[i]; ll ans = a[0], cur = a[0]; for (ll i = 1; i < n; ++i) { if ((a[i] & 1) == (a[i-1] & 1)) { cur = a[i]; } else { cur = max(a[i], cur + a[i]); } ans = max(ans, cur); } cout << ans << endl; } int main() { fastio; int t = 1; cin >> t; while (t--) solve(); return 0; }

finetuned.full: To solve this problem, we need to find the maximum sum of a subarray with alternating parities of adjacent elements. Let's break down the steps: 1. **Understand Alternating Parity**: A sequence has alternating parities if no two consecutive numbers have the same parity. For example, `[1, 2, 3]` has alternating parities, while `[1, 2, 4]` does not because `2` and `4` are both even and adjacent. 2. **Dynamic Programming Approach**: - We can use dynamic programming to keep track of the maximum sum ending at an odd-indexed element and an even-indexed element. - Define `dp_odd[i]` as the maximum sum of a valid subarray ending at index `i` where the last element is odd. - Define `dp_even[i]` as the maximum sum of a valid subarray ending at index `i` where the last element is even. - Initialize `dp_odd[-1] = 0` and `dp_even[-1] = 0` since there's no previous element to consider initially. 3. **Transition**: - For each element `a[i]`, update `dp_odd[i]` and `dp_even[i]` based on the previous values: - If `a[i]` is odd, then `dp_odd[i] = max(dp_odd[i-1], dp_even[i-1]) + a[i]`. - If `a[i]` is even, then `dp_even[i] = max(dp_odd[i-1], dp_even[i-1]) + a[i]`. 4. **Result**: - The result for each test case will be the maximum value from `dp_odd[n-1]` and `dp_even[n-1]` because the subarray could end at either an odd or even index. 5. **Edge Cases**: - Handle arrays with negative numbers carefully. - Ensure the sum does not exceed the given constraints. Let's implement this step-by-step in C++. #include #include #include using namespace std; int solve(const vector& a) { int n = a.size(); int dp_odd = 0; int dp_even = 0; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { if (a[i] % 2 == 0) { dp_even = max(dp_odd, dp_even) + a[i]; } else { dp_odd = max(dp_odd, dp_even) + a[i]; } } return max(dp_odd, dp_even); } int main() { ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); int t; cin >> t; while (t--) { int n; cin >> n; vector a(n); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { cin >> a[i]; } cout << solve(a) << '\n'; } return 0; }

To solve this problem, we need to find the maximum sum of a subarray with alternating parities of adjacent elements. Let's break down the steps:

1. **Understand Alternating Parity**: A sequence has alternating parities if no two consecutive numbers have the same parity. For example, `[1, 2, 3]` has alternating parities, while `[1, 2, 4]` does not because `2` and `4` are both even and adjacent.

2. **Dynamic Programming Approach**:
   - We can use dynamic programming to keep track of the maximum sum ending at an odd-indexed element and an even-indexed element.
   - Define `dp_odd[i]` as the maximum sum of a valid subarray ending at index `i` where the last element is odd.
   - Define `dp_even[i]` as the maximum sum of a valid subarray ending at index `i` where the last element is even.
   - Initialize `dp_odd[-1] = 0` and `dp_even[-1] = 0` since there's no previous element to consider initially.

3. **Transition**:
   - For each element `a[i]`, update `dp_odd[i]` and `dp_even[i]` based on the previous values:
     - If `a[i]` is odd, then `dp_odd[i] = max(dp_odd[i-1], dp_even[i-1]) + a[i]`.
     - If `a[i]` is even, then `dp_even[i] = max(dp_odd[i-1], dp_even[i-1]) + a[i]`.

4. **Result**:
   - The result for each test case will be the maximum value from `dp_odd[n-1]` and `dp_even[n-1]` because the subarray could end at either an odd or even index.

5. **Edge Cases**:
   - Handle arrays with negative numbers carefully.
   - Ensure the sum does not exceed the given constraints.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
You are given a binary matrix $$$b$$$ (all elements of the matrix are $$$0$$$ or $$$1$$$) of $$$n$$$ rows and $$$n$$$ columns.

You need to construct a $$$n$$$ sets $$$A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$$$, for which the following conditions are satisfied:

- Each set is nonempty and consists of distinct integers between $$$1$$$ and $$$n$$$ inclusive.
- All sets are distinct.
- For all pairs $$$(i,j)$$$ satisfying $$$1\leq i, j\leq n$$$, $$$b_{i,j}=1$$$ if and only if $$$A_i\subsetneq A_j$$$. In other words, $$$b_{i, j}$$$ is $$$1$$$ if $$$A_i$$$ is a proper subset of $$$A_j$$$ and $$$0$$$ otherwise.

Set $$$X$$$ is a proper subset of set $$$Y$$$, if $$$X$$$ is a nonempty subset of $$$Y$$$, and $$$X \neq Y$$$.

It's guaranteed that for all test cases in this problem, such $$$n$$$ sets exist. Note that it doesn't mean that such $$$n$$$ sets exist for all possible inputs.

If there are multiple solutions, you can output any of them.

Time limit: 1s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains a single integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1\le t\le 1000$$$) — the number of test cases. The description of test cases follows.

The first line contains a single integer $$$n$$$ ($$$1\le n\le 100$$$).

The following $$$n$$$ lines contain a binary matrix $$$b$$$, the $$$j$$$-th character of $$$i$$$-th line denotes $$$b_{i,j}$$$.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$1000$$$.

It's guaranteed that for all test cases in this problem, such $$$n$$$ sets exist.

Output:
For each test case, output $$$n$$$ lines.

For the $$$i$$$-th line, first output $$$s_i$$$ $$$(1 \le s_i \le n)$$$  — the size of the set $$$A_i$$$. Then, output $$$s_i$$$ distinct integers from $$$1$$$ to $$$n$$$  — the elements of the set $$$A_i$$$.

If there are multiple solutions, you can output any of them.

It's guaranteed that for all test cases in this problem, such $$$n$$$ sets exist.

Note:
In the first test case, we have $$$A_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}, A_2 = \{1, 3\}, A_3 = \{2, 4\}, A_4 = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$$$. Sets $$$A_1, A_2, A_3$$$ are proper subsets of $$$A_4$$$, and also set $$$A_2$$$ is a proper subset of $$$A_1$$$. No other set is a proper subset of any other set.

In the second test case, we have $$$A_1 = \{1\}, A_2 = \{1, 2\}, A_3 = \{1, 2, 3\}$$$. $$$A_1$$$ is a proper subset of $$$A_2$$$ and $$$A_3$$$, and $$$A_2$$$ is a proper subset of $$$A_3$$$.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    2
    4
    0001
    1001
    0001
    0000
    3
    011
    001
    000
    Output:
    3 1 2 3
    2 1 3
    2 2 4
    4 1 2 3 4
    1 1
    2 1 2
    3 1 2 3

<code>
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

void solve() {
    ll n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<string> b(n);
    for (ll i = 0; i < n; ++i) cin >> b[i];
    vector<vll> reach(n, vll(n, 0));
    for (ll i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        for (ll j = 0; j < n; ++j) {
            if (b[i][j] == '1') reach[i][j] = 1;
        }
        reach[i][i] = 1;
    }
    for (ll k = 0; k < n; ++k)
        for (ll i = 0; i < n; ++i)
            for (ll j = 0; j < n; ++j)
                if (reach[i][k] && reach[k][j]) reach[i][j] = 1;
    for (ll i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        vll ans;
        for (ll j = 0; j < n; ++j) {
            if (reach[j][i]) ans.pb(j + 1);
        }
        cout << sz(ans);
        for (ll x : ans) cout << ' ' << x;
        cout << endl;
    }
}

int main() {
    fastio;
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
    return 0;
}

finetuned.full: To solve this problem, we need to construct \( n \) sets \( A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n \) such that each set is nonempty, consists of distinct integers between 1 and \( n \), and satisfies the condition \( b_{i,j} = 1 \) if and only if \( A_i \subsetneq A_j \). ### Key Observations: 1. **Binary Matrix Representation**: The matrix \( b \) represents a partial order on the set \(\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}\). 2. **Proper Subset Condition**: For \( b_{i,j} = 1 \), \( A_i \) must be a proper subset of \( A_j \). 3. **Existence Guarantee**: We are assured that such sets exist for every valid input. ### Approach: We will use the concept of "antichains" and "chains" in poset theory. An antichain is a set of elements where no two elements are comparable, and a chain is a totally ordered subset. **Step-by-Step Construction**: 1. **Topological Ordering**: Since the graph corresponding to the partial order is acyclic (as implied by the existence guarantee), we can perform a topological sort. 2. **Constructing Sets**: Assign numbers to elements based on their topological order. This ensures that if \( i \) comes before \( j \) in the topological order, then \( A_i \subseteq A_j \). ### Detailed Steps: 1. **Read Input**: Parse the input for each test case. 2. **Graph Construction**: Build an adjacency list representation of the graph from the matrix \( b \). 3. **Topological Sort**: Perform a topological sort using Kahn's algorithm. 4. **Assign Numbers**: Assign numbers to nodes during the topological sort. The node with the highest number gets assigned 1, and so on. 5. **Form Sets**: For each node \( i \), create a set \( A_i \) containing all nodes that come before \( i \) in the topological order and node \( i \) itself. ### Edge Cases: - Ensure that each set is non-empty. - Verify that the sets satisfy the proper subset condition. ### Implementation: We'll implement the above steps in C++. Given the constraints, a simple BFS-based topological sort should suffice. #include #include #include #include using namespace std; void solve() { int n; cin >> n; vector> adj(n); vector indegree(n, 0); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { string row; cin >> row; for (int j = 0; j < n; ++j) { if (row[j] == '1') { adj[i].push_back(j); indegree[j]++; } } } queue topoQueue; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { if (indegree[i] == 0) { topoQueue.push(i); } } vector topoOrder; while (!topoQueue.empty()) { int u = topoQueue.front(); topoQueue.pop(); topoOrder.push_back(u); for (int v : adj[u]) { if (--indegree[v] == 0) { topoQueue.push(v); } } } // Topological order exists because the graph is acyclic cout << topoOrder.size() << endl; for (int i : topoOrder) { cout << i + 1 << " "; } cout << endl; } int main() { ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); int t; cin >> t; while (t--) { solve(); } return 0; }

To solve this problem, we need to construct \( n \) sets \( A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n \) such that each set is nonempty, consists of distinct integers between 1 and \( n \), and satisfies the condition \( b_{i,j} = 1 \) if and only if \( A_i \subsetneq A_j \).

### Key Observations:
1. **Binary Matrix Representation**: The matrix \( b \) represents a partial order on the set \(\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}\).
2. **Proper Subset Condition**: For \( b_{i,j} = 1 \), \( A_i \) must be a proper subset of \( A_j \).
3. **Existence Guarantee**: We are assured that such sets exist for every valid input.

### Approach:
We will use the concept of "antichains" and "chains" in poset theory. An antichain is a set of elements where no two elements are comparable, and a chain is a totally ordered subset.

**Step-by-Step Construction**:
1. **Topological Ordering**: Since the graph corresponding to the partial order is acyclic (as implied by the existence guarantee), we can perform a topological sort.
2. **Constructing Sets**: Assign numbers to elements based on their topological order. This ensures that if \( i \) comes before \( j \) in the topological order, then \( A_i \subseteq A_j \).

### Detailed Steps:
1. **Read Input**: Parse the input for each test case.
2. **Graph Construction**: Build an adjacency list representation of the graph from the matrix \( b \).
3. **Topological Sort**: Perform a topological sort using Kahn's algorithm.
4. **Assign Numbers**: Assign numbers to nodes during the topological sort. The node with the highest number gets assigned 1, and so on.
5. **Form Sets**: For each node \( i \), create a set \( A_i \) containing all nodes that come before \( i \) in the topological order and node \( i \) itself.

### Edge Cases:
- Ensure that each set is non-empty.
- Verify that the sets satisfy the proper subset condition.

### Implementation:
We'll implement the above steps in C++. Given the constraints, a simple BFS-based topological sort should suffice.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
Theofanis wants to play video games, however he should also take care of his sister. Since Theofanis is a CS major, he found a way to do both. He will install some cameras in his house in order to make sure his sister is okay.

His house is an undirected graph with $$$n$$$ nodes and $$$m$$$ edges. His sister likes to play at the edges of the graph, so he has to install a camera to at least one endpoint of every edge of the graph. Theofanis wants to find a vertex cover that maximizes the minimum difference between indices of the chosen nodes.

More formally, let $$$a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k$$$ be a vertex cover of the graph. Let the minimum difference between indices of the chosen nodes be the minimum $$$\lvert a_i - a_j \rvert$$$ (where $$$i \neq j$$$) out of the nodes that you chose. If $$$k = 1$$$ then we assume that the minimum difference between indices of the chosen nodes is $$$n$$$.

Can you find the maximum possible minimum difference between indices of the chosen nodes over all vertex covers?

Time limit: 7s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
The first line contains a single integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^4$$$) — the number of test cases.

The first line of each test case contains two integers $$$n$$$ and $$$m$$$ ($$$1 \le n \le 10^{5}, 1 \le m \le 2 \cdot 10^{5}$$$) — the number of nodes and the number of edges.

The $$$i$$$-th of the following $$$m$$$ lines in the test case contains two positive integers $$$u_i$$$ and $$$v_i$$$ ($$$1 \le u_i,v_i \le n$$$), meaning that there exists an edge between them in the graph.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$10^{5}$$$.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$m$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$2 \cdot 10^{5}$$$.

Output:
For each test case, print the maximum minimum difference between indices of the chosen nodes over all vertex covers.

Note:
In the first test case, we can install cameras at nodes $$$1$$$, $$$3$$$, and $$$7$$$, so the answer is $$$2$$$.

In the second test case, we can install only one camera at node $$$1$$$, so the answer is $$$3$$$.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    3
    7 6
    1 2
    1 3
    1 4
    1 6
    2 3
    5 7
    3 3
    1 2
    1 3
    1 1
    2 4
    1 2
    1 2
    2 1
    1 1
    Output:
    2
    3
    2

The first two ideas — greedy sliding window and flow-based reduction — blew up because vertex cover is NP-complete on arbitrary graphs, so any exact polynomial reduction would imply P=NP. But that third idea, framing feasibility for a candidate distance d as a satisfiability problem with clever pairwise exclusions, actually checks out. The “no two close” is trickier: for any pair of vertices i and j with |i - j| < d, we cannot choose both, so we require (NOT choose_i OR NOT choose_j). That’s a 2-SAT problem! That’s equivalent to saying: if we choose i, then all vertices from i+1 to i+d-1 must be false. In 2-SAT, that translates to implications: choose_i → NOT choose_{i+1}, choose_i → NOT choose_{i+2}, …, choose_i → NOT choose_{i+d-1}. But that’s still O(n*d) implications, which is too large if d is big. However, we can chain these implications! If we add choose_i → NOT choose_{i+1} and choose_{i+1} → NOT choose_{i+2}, then by transitivity choose_i implies NOT choose_{i+2} as well. Actually, a cleaner way: treat the chosen vertices as a set with spacing at least d. That’s equivalent to saying that in any block of d consecutive vertices, at most one can be chosen. That’s a classic at-most-one constraint, which can be encoded in 2-SAT using a chain of implications with auxiliary variables. For each block [L, R] of length d, we need at most one chosen. We can use the sequential encoding: for each block, introduce auxiliary variables s_k meaning “no vertex among the first k in this block is chosen”. Then we have implications: if vertex_k is chosen, then s_{k-1} is false; if s_{k-1} is false, then s_k is false; and we enforce s_0 = true, s_d = false. But that’s per block, and blocks overlap, so the total number of auxiliary variables becomes O(n). However, we can reuse auxiliary variables across blocks because the at-most-one constraint is global: it’s not per block, but for all pairs within distance < d. Actually, the condition “no two within distance < d” is equivalent to “in every contiguous segment of d vertices, at most one is chosen”. So we need to enforce at-most-one for every sliding window of size d. That’s exactly what the sequential encoding can do with O(n) auxiliary variables and O(n) implications if we do it cleverly. Let’s derive it properly. For each vertex i, we have a Boolean variable x_i (true if chosen). We need two sets of constraints: (1) For each edge (u, v): (x_u OR x_v). #include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

const int N = 1 << 20;

struct SAT {
    vector<int> g[N << 1], stk;
    int dep[N << 1], low[N << 1], scc_id[N << 1];
    int n, _id, _t;
    bool is[N];

    SAT() {}

    void init(int _n) {
        n = _n, _id = _t = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < 2 * n; ++i) {
            g[i].clear();
            dep[i] = scc_id[i] = -1;
        }
        stk.clear();
    }

    void add_edge(int x, int y) {
        g[x].pb(y);
    }

    int rev(int i) {
        return i < n ? i + n : i - n;
    }

    void add_ifthen(int x, int y) {
        add_clause(rev(x), y);
    }

    void add_clause(int x, int y) {
        add_edge(rev(x), y);
        add_edge(rev(y), x);
    }

    void dfs(int i) {
        dep[i] = low[i] = _t++;
        stk.pb(i);
        for (int j : g[i]) {
            if (scc_id[j] == -1) {
                if (dep[j] == -1) dfs(j);
                low[i] = min(low[i], low[j]);
            }
        }
        if (low[i] == dep[i]) {
            int id = _id++, x;
            do {
                x = stk.back();
                stk.pob();
                scc_id[x] = id;
            } while (x != i);
        }
    }

    bool solve() {
        for (int i = 0; i < 2 * n; ++i) {
            if (dep[i] == -1) dfs(i);
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            if (scc_id[i] == scc_id[i + n]) return false;
            is[i] = (scc_id[i] < scc_id[i + n]);
        }
        return true;
    }
} sat;

void solve() {
    int n, m;
    cin >> n >> m;
    vector<pair<int, int>> edge(m);
    for (auto &[u, v] : edge) {
        cin >> u >> v;
        --u, --v;
    }

    auto add = [&](int l, int r, int id) {
        for (l += n, r += n; l < r; l >>= 1, r >>= 1) {
            if (l & 1) {
                sat.add_clause(sat.rev(id), sat.rev(l));
                ++l;
            }
            if (r & 1) {
                --r;
                sat.add_clause(sat.rev(id), sat.rev(r));
            }
        }
    };

    auto ask = [&](int num) {
        sat.init(2 * n);
        for (int i = 1; i < n; ++i) {
            sat.add_ifthen(i << 1, i);
            sat.add_ifthen(i << 1 | 1, i);
        }
        for (auto [u, v] : edge) {
            sat.add_clause(u + n, v + n);
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            add(max(i - num + 1, 0), i, i + n);
            add(i + 1, min(i + num, n), i + n);
        }
        return sat.solve();
    };

    int l = 1, r = n + 1;
    while (r - l > 1) {
        int mid = (l + r) >> 1;
        if (ask(mid)) l = mid;
        else r = mid;
    }
    cout << l << '\n';
}

int main() {
    fastio;
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
    return 0;
}

finetuned.full: To solve this problem, we need to maximize the minimum distance between chosen nodes in a vertex cover of the given graph. Here's the step-by-step approach: 1. **Understanding Vertex Cover**: A vertex cover is a set of vertices such that each edge of the graph is incident to at least one vertex in the set. We need to choose a vertex cover that maximizes the smallest distance between any two chosen vertices. 2. **Graph Representation**: The graph is represented as an adjacency list since the number of edges can be up to \(2 \times 10^5\). 3. **Greedy Strategy**: To maximize the minimum difference, we can start by sorting the nodes based on their degree. Nodes with higher degrees might help in covering more edges efficiently. However, we need to ensure that the chosen nodes form a valid vertex cover and maintain the maximum minimum distance. 4. **Binary Search on Distance**: Instead of directly choosing nodes, we can think about placing nodes at positions that maximize the minimum distance. This suggests using binary search on the distance. 5. **Simulated Annealing**: Given the complexity of finding an exact optimal solution, we can use a heuristic approach like simulated annealing to approximate the best vertex cover. Let's outline the steps in more detail: - For each test case, read the graph. - Sort nodes based on their degree. - Implement a greedy selection process to pick nodes that cover the most uncovered edges while maintaining the maximum minimum distance. - Use binary search to adjust the distance threshold dynamically. This approach balances between exploring different configurations and ensuring efficiency within the given constraints. #include using namespace std; #define ll long long #define pb push_back #define mp make_pair #define F first #define S second #define MOD 1000000007 const int N = 1e5 + 7; vector adj[N]; int deg[N]; void bfs(int src, vector& visited) { queue q; q.push(src); visited[src] = true; while (!q.empty()) { int u = q.front(); q.pop(); for (int v : adj[u]) { if (!visited[v]) { visited[v] = true; q.push(v); } } } } bool isValid(vector& selected, int min_dist) { int n = selected.size(); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { for (int j = i + 1; j < n; ++j) { if (abs(selected[i] - selected[j]) < min_dist) return false; } } return true; } int solve(int n, int m) { for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) adj[i].clear(); memset(deg, 0, sizeof(deg)); for (int i = 1; i <= m; ++i) { int u, v; cin >> u >> v; adj[u].pb(v); adj[v].pb(u); deg[u]++; deg[v]++; } vector selected; for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) { if (deg[i] == 1 && !selected.empty()) { bool covered = false; for (int j : selected) { if (adj[i][0] == j || adj[i][1] == j) { covered = true; break; } } if (!covered) { selected.pb(i); } } else if (deg[i] > 1) { selected.pb(i); } } sort(selected.begin(), selected.end()); reverse(selected.begin(), selected.end()); int lo = 1, hi = n; while (lo < hi) { int mid = (lo + hi + 1) / 2; if (isValid(selected, mid)) { lo = mid; } else { hi = mid - 1; } } return lo; } int main() { ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); int t; cin >> t; while (t--) { cout << solve(n, m) << '\n'; } return 0; }

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
Each item in the game has a level. The higher the level is, the higher basic parameters the item has. We shall consider only the following basic parameters: attack (atk), defense (def) and resistance to different types of impact (res).

Each item belongs to one class. In this problem we will only consider three of such classes: weapon, armor, orb.

Besides, there's a whole new world hidden inside each item. We can increase an item's level travelling to its world. We can also capture the so-called residents in the Item World

Residents are the creatures that live inside items. Each resident gives some bonus to the item in which it is currently located. We will only consider residents of types: gladiator (who improves the item's atk), sentry (who improves def) and physician (who improves res).

Each item has the size parameter. The parameter limits the maximum number of residents that can live inside an item. We can move residents between items. Within one moment of time we can take some resident from an item and move it to some other item if it has a free place for a new resident. We cannot remove a resident from the items and leave outside — any of them should be inside of some item at any moment of time.

Laharl has a certain number of items. He wants to move the residents between items so as to equip himself with weapon, armor and a defensive orb. The weapon's atk should be largest possible in the end. Among all equipping patterns containing weapon's maximum atk parameter we should choose the ones where the armor’s def parameter is the largest possible. Among all such equipment patterns we should choose the one where the defensive orb would have the largest possible res parameter. Values of the parameters def and res of weapon, atk and res of armor and atk and def of orb are indifferent for Laharl.

Find the optimal equipment pattern Laharl can get.

Time limit: 2s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
The first line contains number n (3 ≤ n ≤ 100) — representing how many items Laharl has.

Then follow n lines. Each line contains description of an item. The description has the following form: "name class atk def res size" — the item's name, class, basic attack, defense and resistance parameters and its size correspondingly.

- name and class are strings and atk, def, res and size are integers.
- name consists of lowercase Latin letters and its length can range from 1 to 10, inclusive.
- class can be "weapon", "armor" or "orb".
- 0 ≤ atk, def, res ≤ 1000.
- 1 ≤ size ≤ 10.

It is guaranteed that Laharl has at least one item of each class.

The next line contains an integer k (1 ≤ k ≤ 1000) — the number of residents.

Then k lines follow. Each of them describes a resident. A resident description looks like: "name type bonus home" — the resident's name, his type, the number of points the resident adds to the item's corresponding parameter and the name of the item which currently contains the resident.

- name, type and home are strings and bonus is an integer.
- name consists of lowercase Latin letters and its length can range from 1 to 10, inclusive.
- type may be "gladiator", "sentry" or "physician".
- 1 ≤ bonus ≤ 100.

It is guaranteed that the number of residents in each item does not exceed the item's size.

The names of all items and residents are pairwise different.

All words and numbers in the input are separated by single spaces.

Output:
Print on the first line the name of the weapon in the optimal equipping pattern; then print the number of residents the weapon contains; then print the residents' names.

Print on the second and third lines in the same form the names of the armor and defensive orb as well as the residents they contain.

Use single spaces for separation.

If there are several possible solutions, print any of them.

Note:
In the second sample we have no free space inside the items, therefore we cannot move the residents between them.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    4
    sword weapon 10 2 3 2
    pagstarmor armor 0 15 3 1
    iceorb orb 3 2 13 2
    longbow weapon 9 1 2 1
    5
    mike gladiator 5 longbow
    bobby sentry 6 pagstarmor
    petr gladiator 7 iceorb
    teddy physician 6 sword
    blackjack sentry 8 sword
    Output:
    sword 2 petr mike
    pagstarmor 1 blackjack
    iceorb 2 teddy bobby

  Example 2:
    Input:
    4
    sword weapon 10 2 3 2
    pagstarmor armor 0 15 3 1
    iceorb orb 3 2 13 2
    longbow weapon 9 1 2 1
    6
    mike gladiator 5 longbow
    bobby sentry 6 pagstarmor
    petr gladiator 7 iceorb
    teddy physician 6 sword
    blackjack sentry 8 sword
    joe physician 6 iceorb
    Output:
    longbow 1 mike
    pagstarmor 1 bobby
    iceorb 2 petr joe

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

struct Resident {
    string name;
    string type;
    ll value;
    string item;
};

unordered_map<string, vector<string>> items;
unordered_map<string, vector<Resident>> equipped;
unordered_map<string, string> liesIn;

string searchBest(const string& iType, int number, const string& rType, bool countResidents) {
    ll best = 0;
    string ret = "";
    for (auto& p : items) {
        const string& item = p.first;
        const vector<string>& params = p.second;
        if (params[0] == iType) {
            ll val = stoll(params[number]);
            if (countResidents) {
                for (const Resident& resid : equipped[item]) {
                    if (resid.type == rType) {
                        val += resid.value;
                    }
                }
            }
            if (val > best) {
                best = val;
                ret = item;
            }
        }
    }
    return ret;
}

void printItem(const string& item) {
    cout << item << " " << equipped[item].size();
    for (const Resident& r : equipped[item]) {
        cout << " " << r.name;
    }
    cout << "\n";
}

string searchFor(const string& iType, int number, const vector<Resident>& might) {
    vll pSum = {0};
    for (const Resident& x : might) {
        pSum.pb(pSum.back() + x.value);
    }
    while (pSum.size() < 11) {
        pSum.pb(pSum.back());
    }
    ll bestVal = 0;
    for (auto& p : items) {
        const vector<string>& params = p.second;
        if (params[0] == iType) {
            ll val = stoll(params[number]) + pSum[stoll(params[4])];
            if (val > bestVal) {
                bestVal = val;
            }
        }
    }
    for (auto& p : items) {
        const string& item = p.first;
        const vector<string>& params = p.second;
        if (params[0] == iType) {
            ll val = stoll(params[number]) + pSum[stoll(params[4])];
            if (val == bestVal) {
                int cap = stoll(params[4]);
                int limit = min(cap, (int)might.size());
                for (int i = 0; i < limit; ++i) {
                    Resident want = might[i];
                    string oldItem = liesIn[want.name];
                    auto& oldEquipped = equipped[oldItem];
                    oldEquipped.erase(remove_if(oldEquipped.begin(), oldEquipped.end(),
                        [&](const Resident& r) { return r.name == want.name; }), oldEquipped.end());
                    liesIn[want.name] = item;
                    if ((int)equipped[item].size() == cap) {
                        Resident rm = equipped[item][0];
                        liesIn[rm.name] = want.item;
                        equipped[want.item].insert(equipped[want.item].begin(), rm);
                        equipped[item].erase(equipped[item].begin());
                    }
                    equipped[item].pb(want);
                }
                return item;
            }
        }
    }
    return "";
}

void rel(const string& item) {
    while ((int)equipped[item].size() > stoll(items[item][4])) {
        Resident toDelete = equipped[item][0];
        for (auto& p : items) {
            const string& other = p.first;
            if ((int)equipped[other].size() < stoll(p.second[4])) {
                liesIn[toDelete.name] = other;
                equipped[other].pb(toDelete);
                break;
            }
        }
        equipped[item].erase(equipped[item].begin());
    }
}

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        string itemName;
        cin >> itemName;
        vector<string> params;
        string token;
        for (int j = 0; j < 5; ++j) {
            cin >> token;
            params.pb(token);
        }
        items[itemName] = params;
        equipped[itemName] = {};
    }

    int k;
    cin >> k;
    vector<Resident> residents(k);
    for (int i = 0; i < k; ++i) {
        string name, type, valueStr, item;
        cin >> name >> type >> valueStr >> item;
        ll value = stoll(valueStr);
        residents[i] = {name, type, value, item};
        equipped[item].pb(residents[i]);
        liesIn[name] = item;
    }

    bool canSwap = false;
    for (auto& p : equipped) {
        const string& name = p.first;
        if ((int)p.second.size() < stoll(items[name][4])) {
            canSwap = true;
            break;
        }
    }

    if (canSwap) {
        vector<Resident> glads, sentries, phys;
        for (const Resident& r : residents) {
            if (r.type == "gladiator") glads.pb(r);
            else if (r.type == "sentry") sentries.pb(r);
            else if (r.type == "physician") phys.pb(r);
        }
        sort(glads.begin(), glads.end(), [](const Resident& a, const Resident& b) { return a.value > b.value; });
        sort(sentries.begin(), sentries.end(), [](const Resident& a, const Resident& b) { return a.value > b.value; });
        sort(phys.begin(), phys.end(), [](const Resident& a, const Resident& b) { return a.value > b.value; });

        string wp = searchFor("weapon", 1, glads);
        string ar = searchFor("armor", 2, sentries);
        string orb = searchFor("orb", 3, phys);

        rel(wp);
        rel(ar);
        rel(orb);

        printItem(wp);
        printItem(ar);
        printItem(orb);
    } else {
        printItem(searchBest("weapon", 1, "gladiator", true));
        printItem(searchBest("armor", 2, "sentry", true));
        printItem(searchBest("orb", 3, "physician", true));
    }
}

int main() {
    fastio;
    solve();
    return 0;
}
