<code>
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vll arr(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        cin >> arr[i];
    }
    
    ll r = 0;
    int mid = -1;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        r ^= arr[i];
        if (r == 0 && i % 2 == 0) {
            mid = i;
        }
    }
    
    if (r) {
        cout << "NO\n";
    } else if (n % 2 == 1) {
        cout << "YES\n";
        vector<int> ans;
        for (int i = 1; i < n - 1; i += 2) {
            ans.pb(i);
        }
        for (int i = n - 2; i >= 1; i -= 2) {
            ans.pb(i);
        }
        cout << ans.size() << "\n";
        for (size_t j = 0; j < ans.size(); ++j) {
            cout << ans[j] << " \n"[j + 1 == ans.size()];
        }
    } else {
        if (mid == -1) {
            cout << "NO\n";
        } else {
            cout << "YES\n";
            vector<int> ans;
            for (int i = 1; i < mid; i += 2) {
                ans.pb(i);
            }
            for (int i = mid - 1; i >= 1; i -= 2) {
                ans.pb(i);
            }
            for (int i = mid + 2; i < n - 1; i += 2) {
                ans.pb(i);
            }
            for (int i = n - 2; i > mid + 1; i -= 2) {
                ans.pb(i);
            }
            cout << ans.size() << "\n";
            for (size_t j = 0; j < ans.size(); ++j) {
                cout << ans[j] << " \n"[j + 1 == ans.size()];
            }
        }
    }
}

int main() {
    fastio;
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
    return 0;
}
</code>

I'll use a hash set of visited states to avoid cycles, and a queue. I stop when I hit the all-zero state or exhaust the queue up to depth n.

From a given state, there are n-2 possible operations. Each operation produces one new state (though many may coincide or cycle back). In the worst case, if every operation at every step produces a distinct new state, the number of states at depth d is (n-2)^d. Summing over d=0 to n gives roughly (n-2)^n states — still enormous.

finetuned.full: To solve this problem, we need to understand how the XOR operation affects the sequence when applied to three consecutive elements. Let's denote the operation on position \( i \) as flipping \( a_i, a_{i+1}, a_{i+2} \): \[ a_i' = a_i \oplus a_{i+1} \oplus a_{i+2} \] \[ a_{i+1}' = a_i \oplus a_{i+1} \oplus a_{i+2} \] \[ a_{i+2}' = a_i \oplus a_{i+1} \oplus a_{i+2} \] This means that applying the operation twice on the same set of three elements will return the original values because \( x \oplus x \oplus x = x \). Given the constraint that we can perform up to \( n \) operations, we need to determine if we can make all elements zero. ### Key Observations: 1. **Parity of Flips**: Each operation flips three bits. Since the XOR of three bits is either 0 or 1, each operation toggles the parity of the number of 1s in the affected triplet. 2. **Effect on Sequence**: Applying the operation on every possible triplet once would affect every bit at least once. 3. **Goal**: We want to achieve a sequence of all zeros. ### Strategy: 1. **Check Initial Parity**: If the initial number of 1s in the sequence is odd, it’s impossible to make all elements zero because each operation flips an odd number of bits, maintaining the overall parity. 2. **Greedy Approach**: For sequences with even number of 1s, we can try to find a pattern or specific operations to zero out the sequence. Let’s implement this step-by-step: 1. Count the number of 1s in the sequence. 2. If the count is odd, output "NO". 3. If the count is even, try to find a sequence of operations that zero out the array. This might involve alternating patterns or targeted operations. #include #include #include using namespace std; void solve() { int n; cin >> n; vector a(n); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { cin >> a[i]; } // Count the number of 1s int ones = count(a.begin(), a.end(), 1); // If number of 1s is odd, we cannot make all zeros if (ones % 2 != 0) { cout << "NO" << endl; return; } // If number of 1s is even, we can always make all zeros cout << "YES" << endl; cout << n / 3 << endl; for (int i = 0; i < n / 3; ++i) { cout << (i + 1) * 3 - 2 << " "; } cout << endl; } int main() { ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); int t; cin >> t; while (t--) { solve(); } return 0; }

To solve this problem, we need to understand how the XOR operation affects the sequence when applied to three consecutive elements. Let's denote the operation on position \( i \) as flipping \( a_i, a_{i+1}, a_{i+2} \):

\[ a_i' = a_i \oplus a_{i+1} \oplus a_{i+2} \]
\[ a_{i+1}' = a_i \oplus a_{i+1} \oplus a_{i+2} \]
\[ a_{i+2}' = a_i \oplus a_{i+1} \oplus a_{i+2} \]

This means that applying the operation twice on the same set of three elements will return the original values because \( x \oplus x \oplus x = x \).

Given the constraint that we can perform up to \( n \) operations, we need to determine if we can make all elements zero. 

### Key Observations:
1. **Parity of Flips**: Each operation flips three bits. Since the XOR of three bits is either 0 or 1, each operation toggles the parity of the number of 1s in the affected triplet.
2. **Effect on Sequence**: Applying the operation on every possible triplet once would affect every bit at least once.
3. **Goal**: We want to achieve a sequence of all zeros.

### Strategy:
1. **Check Initial Parity**: If the initial number of 1s in the sequence is odd, it’s impossible to make all elements zero because each operation flips an odd number of bits, maintaining the overall parity.
2. **Greedy Approach**: For sequences with even number of 1s, we can try to find a pattern or specific operations to zero out the sequence.

Let’s implement this step-by-step:

1. Count the number of 1s in the sequence.
2. If the count is odd, output "NO".
3. If the count is even, try to find a sequence of operations that zero out the array. This might involve alternating patterns or targeted operations.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
Define a cyclic sequence of size $$$n$$$ as an array $$$s$$$ of length $$$n$$$, in which $$$s_n$$$ is adjacent to $$$s_1$$$.

Muxii has a ring represented by a cyclic sequence $$$a$$$ of size $$$n$$$.

However, the ring itself hates equal adjacent elements. So if two adjacent elements in the sequence are equal at any time, one of them will be erased immediately. The sequence doesn't contain equal adjacent elements initially.

Muxii can perform the following operation until the sequence becomes empty:

- Choose an element in $$$a$$$ and erase it.

For example, if ring is $$$[1, 2, 4, 2, 3, 2]$$$, and Muxii erases element $$$4$$$, then ring would erase one of the elements equal to $$$2$$$, and the ring will become $$$[1, 2, 3, 2]$$$.

Muxii wants to find the maximum number of operations he could perform.

Note that in a ring of size $$$1$$$, its only element isn't considered adjacent to itself (so it's not immediately erased).

Time limit: 1s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains a single integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1\leq t\leq 100$$$) — the number of test cases. The description of test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $$$n$$$ ($$$1\leq n\leq 100$$$) — the size of the cyclic sequence.

The second line of each test case contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n$$$ ($$$1\leq a_i\leq n$$$) — the sequence itself.

It's guaranteed that $$$a_i\ne a_{i+1}$$$ for $$$1\leq i<n$$$.

It's guaranteed that $$$a_n\ne a_1$$$ when $$$n>1$$$.

Output:
For each test case, output a single integer — the maximum number of operations Muxii can perform.

Note:
In the first test case, you can erase the second element first, then erase the remaining elements one by one in any order. In total, you can perform the operation $$$4$$$ times. Note that if you erase the first element first, then the sequence will be turned into $$$[2,3,2]$$$ and then immediately become $$$[2,3]$$$.

In the second test case, you can erase the first element first, then the sequence becomes $$$[2,1]$$$. Then you can erase all remaining elements one by one in any order.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    3
    4
    1 2 3 2
    4
    1 2 1 2
    1
    1
    Output:
    4
    3
    1

reference.full: Alright, let's start by understanding the rules. We have a cyclic sequence with no equal adjacent elements initially. When we delete an element, if its removal causes two equal elements to become adjacent (because the neighbors are now next to each other), one of those equal elements is erased immediately as an automatic reaction. Muxii's goal is to maximize the number of operations *he* performs — i.e., the number of times he chooses and erases an element manually. We need to find the maximum possible manual deletions before the sequence becomes empty. First, small cases: - n = 1: Only one element, no equal adjacent possible, so he can erase it manually → 1 operation. - n = 2: Two different elements. If he erases one, the other remains alone (no adjacent equal because size 1), so he can erase both manually → 2 operations. Now, think about n = 3. Example: 1 2 3. All distinct. If he erases any element, the remaining two are adjacent in a cycle of size 2, and they are different, so no auto-erase. Then he can erase the remaining two manually. So total 3 operations. Then delete 2 → 3 4 (no equal adjacent). Then delete 3 → 4 (size 1). So yes, 4 operations. Now, what if there are duplicates? Example given: 1 2 3 2 → output 4. finetuned.full: To solve this problem, we need to determine the maximum number of operations Muxii can perform on a cyclic sequence such that no two adjacent elements are equal. Let's break down the steps: 1. **Understanding the Problem**: - We have a cyclic sequence \( a \) of size \( n \). - Initially, there are no equal adjacent elements. - When Muxii erases an element, if the resulting sequence has two equal adjacent elements, one of them is erased immediately. - We need to maximize the number of operations. 2. **Observations**: - Each time Muxii erases an element, the sequence can potentially shrink. - The key is to understand how many elements can be erased before the sequence becomes invalid (i.e., two equal adjacent elements appear). 3. **Strategy**: - For each element, consider the possibility of erasing it and see how many subsequent erasures can happen without creating equal adjacent elements. - This involves simulating the process and counting the number of valid erasures. 4. **Constraints and Edge Cases**: - \( n \) can be up to 100, so brute-force simulation might be feasible. - The guarantee ensures no initial adjacent duplicates, simplifying some checks. 5. **Implementation**: - For each position \( i \), simulate erasing \( a[i] \) and count how many valid erasures follow. - Use a stack-like approach to keep track of potential future erasures. Let's implement this step-by-step: 1. **Simulation Function**: - Start from index \( i \). - Erase \( a[i] \) and maintain a deque to track the sequence. - While the deque has elements and the last two elements are not equal, pop the last element. - Count the number of pops and return the total count. 2. **Main Loop**: - Iterate over all indices and sum the results. #include #include #include using namespace std; int maxOperations(vector& a) { int n = a.size(); int ans = 0; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { deque dq(a.begin(), a.end()); dq.erase(dq.begin() + i); while (!dq.empty()) { if (dq.size() == 1 || dq.front() != dq.back()) { dq.pop_back(); } else { break; } } ans += dq.size(); } return ans; } int main() { ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(0); int t; cin >> t; while (t--) { int n; cin >> n; vector a(n); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { cin >> a[i]; } cout << maxOperations(a) << endl; } return 0; }

To solve this problem, we need to determine the maximum number of operations Muxii can perform on a cyclic sequence such that no two adjacent elements are equal. Let's break down the steps:

1. **Understanding the Problem**:
   - We have a cyclic sequence \( a \) of size \( n \).
   - Initially, there are no equal adjacent elements.
   - When Muxii erases an element, if the resulting sequence has two equal adjacent elements, one of them is erased immediately.
   - We need to maximize the number of operations.

2. **Observations**:
   - Each time Muxii erases an element, the sequence can potentially shrink.
   - The key is to understand how many elements can be erased before the sequence becomes invalid (i.e., two equal adjacent elements appear).

3. **Strategy**:
   - For each element, consider the possibility of erasing it and see how many subsequent erasures can happen without creating equal adjacent elements.
   - This involves simulating the process and counting the number of valid erasures.

4. **Constraints and Edge Cases**:
   - \( n \) can be up to 100, so brute-force simulation might be feasible.
   - The guarantee ensures no initial adjacent duplicates, simplifying some checks.

5. **Implementation**:
   - For each position \( i \), simulate erasing \( a[i] \) and count how many valid erasures follow.
   - Use a stack-like approach to keep track of potential future erasures.

Let's implement this step-by-step:

1. **Simulation Function**:
   - Start from index \( i \).
   - Erase \( a[i] \) and maintain a deque to track the sequence.
   - While the deque has elements and the last two elements are not equal, pop the last element.
   - Count the number of pops and return the total count.

2. **Main Loop**:
   - Iterate over all indices and sum the results.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
This is the hard version of the problem. The difference between the versions is that the easy version has no swap operations. You can make hacks only if all versions of the problem are solved.

Pikachu is a cute and friendly pokémon living in the wild pikachu herd.

But it has become known recently that infamous team R wanted to steal all these pokémon! Pokémon trainer Andrew decided to help Pikachu to build a pokémon army to resist.

First, Andrew counted all the pokémon — there were exactly $$$n$$$ pikachu. The strength of the $$$i$$$-th pokémon is equal to $$$a_i$$$, and all these numbers are distinct.

As an army, Andrew can choose any non-empty subsequence of pokemons. In other words, Andrew chooses some array $$$b$$$ from $$$k$$$ indices such that $$$1 \le b_1 < b_2 < \dots < b_k \le n$$$, and his army will consist of pokémons with forces $$$a_{b_1}, a_{b_2}, \dots, a_{b_k}$$$.

The strength of the army is equal to the alternating sum of elements of the subsequence; that is, $$$a_{b_1} - a_{b_2} + a_{b_3} - a_{b_4} + \dots$$$.

Andrew is experimenting with pokémon order. He performs $$$q$$$ operations. In $$$i$$$-th operation Andrew swaps $$$l_i$$$-th and $$$r_i$$$-th pokémon.

Andrew wants to know the maximal stregth of the army he can achieve with the initial pokémon placement. He also needs to know the maximal strength after each operation.

Help Andrew and the pokémon, or team R will realize their tricky plan!

Time limit: 2s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
Each test contains multiple test cases.

The first line contains one positive integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^3$$$) denoting the number of test cases. Description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains two integers $$$n$$$ and $$$q$$$ ($$$1 \le n \le 3 \cdot 10^5, 0 \le q \le 3 \cdot 10^5$$$) denoting the number of pokémon and number of operations respectively.

The second line contains $$$n$$$ distinct positive integers $$$a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$$$ ($$$1 \le a_i \le n$$$) denoting the strengths of the pokémon.

$$$i$$$-th of the last $$$q$$$ lines contains two positive integers $$$l_i$$$ and $$$r_i$$$ ($$$1 \le l_i \le r_i \le n$$$) denoting the indices of pokémon that were swapped in the $$$i$$$-th operation.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$3 \cdot 10^5$$$, and the sum of $$$q$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$3 \cdot 10^5$$$.

Output:
For each test case, print $$$q+1$$$ integers: the maximal strength of army before the swaps and after each swap.

Note:
Let's look at the third test case:

Initially we can build an army in such way: [1 2 5 4 3 6 7], its strength will be $$$5-3+7=9$$$.

After first operation we can build an army in such way: [2 1 5 4 3 6 7], its strength will be $$$2-1+5-3+7=10$$$.

After second operation we can build an army in such way: [2 1 5 4 3 7 6], its strength will be $$$2-1+5-3+7=10$$$.

After third operation we can build an army in such way: [2 1 4 5 3 7 6], its strength will be $$$2-1+5-3+7=10$$$.

After forth operation we can build an army in such way: [1 2 4 5 3 7 6], its strength will be $$$5-3+7=9$$$.

After all operations we can build an army in such way: [1 4 2 5 3 7 6], its strength will be $$$4-2+5-3+7=11$$$.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    3
    3 1
    1 3 2
    1 2
    2 2
    1 2
    1 2
    1 2
    7 5
    1 2 5 4 3 6 7
    1 2
    6 7
    3 4
    1 2
    2 3
    Output:
    3
    4
    2
    2
    2
    9
    10
    10
    10
    9
    11

reference.full:
<think>
Okay so we have an array a of n distinct integers, and we need to find the maximum alternating sum of any non-empty subsequence. The subsequence must preserve order. The alternating sum is a_{b1} - a_{b2} + a_{b3} - a_{b4} + ... . We also have q swap operations, and after each swap we need to output the new maximum. The constraints: sum of n over all test cases ≤ 3×10^5, sum of q ≤ 3×10^5. Time limit 2 seconds, so budget roughly 2×10^8 operations.

I'll try the DP approach with sign tracking. Define dp[i][1] as the maximum alternating sum achievable using elements from the first i positions, where the last taken element was added with a plus sign. dp[i][-1] is the same but the last taken element was subtracted. The recurrence: dp[i][1] = max(dp[i-1][1], dp[i-1][-1] + a[i]), and dp[i][-1] = max(dp[i-1][-1], dp[i-1][1] - a[i]). Base: dp[0][1] = -infinity (or 0 if we allow empty? but army must be non-empty, so we need to handle first pick carefully). Actually, we can start with dp[0][1] = 0 meaning we haven't picked anything yet and the next pick will be positive, and dp[0][-1] = -infinity. Then at each step we can either skip a[i] or take it with the appropriate sign.

Let me trace Example 1, first test case: n=3, q=1, a=[1,3,2], swap (1,2). Initial array: [1,3,2].
Initialize: dp[0][1]=0, dp[0][-1]=-inf.
i=1, a[1]=1:
  dp[1][1] = max(dp[0][1], dp[0][-1]+1) = max(0, -inf+1) = 0
  dp[1][-1] = max(dp[0][-1], dp[0][1]-1) = max(-inf, 0-1) = -1
i=2, a[2]=3:
  dp[2][1] = max(dp[1][1], dp[1][-1]+3) = max(0, -1+3) = max(0,2) = 2
  dp[2][-1] = max(dp[1][-1], dp[1][1]-3) = max(-1, 0-3) = -1
i=3, a[3]=2:
  dp[3][1] = max(dp[2][1], dp[2][-1]+2) = max(2, -1+2) = max(2,1) = 2
  dp[3][-1] = max(dp[2][-1], dp[2][1]-2) = max(-1, 2-2) = max(-1,0) = 0

The maximum alternating sum is max(dp[3][1], dp[3][-1]) = max(2,0) = 2. But the expected output for initial array is 3. The subsequence [3] gives 3, which is better. My DP gave 2. Let me check: dp[2][1]=2 came from taking a[1]=1 with minus? Wait, dp[1][-1]=-1 means we took a[1] with minus sign, so the sequence so far is just [-1]? That doesn't make sense — the first element should be positive. The recurrence allows taking a[i] with minus even if nothing was taken before, because dp[i-1][1] could be 0 (empty sequence) and then dp[i][-1] = 0 - a[i] = -a[i]. That's wrong — the first element must be positive. So the DP state needs to track whether we've taken at least one element. Let me adjust: dp[i][0] = max sum ending with no element taken (always 0), dp[i][1] = max sum ending with a positive pick, dp[i][-1] = max sum ending with a negative pick. Then dp[i][1] = max(dp[i-1][1], dp[i-1][-1] + a[i]), but also we can start a new sequence: dp[i][1] = max(dp[i][1], a[i]). And dp[i][-1] = max(dp[i-1][-1], dp[i-1][1] - a[i]), but we cannot start with a negative, so no standalone -a[i].

Let me re-trace with this fix. a=[1,3,2].
dp[0][0]=0, dp[0][1]=-inf, dp[0][-1]=-inf.
i=1, a[1]=1:
  dp[1][1] = max(dp[0][1], dp[0][-1]+1, 1) = max(-inf, -inf+1, 1) = 1
  dp[1][-1] = max(dp[0][-1], dp[0][1]-1) = max(-inf, -inf-1) = -inf (can't start with minus)
i=2, a[2]=3:
  dp[2][1] = max(dp[1][1], dp[1][-1]+3, 3) = max(1, -inf+3, 3) = 3
  dp[2][-1] = max(dp[1][-1], dp[1][1]-3) = max(-inf, 1-3) = -2
i=3, a[3]=2:
  dp[3][1] = max(dp[2][1], dp[2][-1]+2, 2) = max(3, -2+2, 2) = max(3,0,2) = 3
  dp[3][-1] = max(dp[2][-1], dp[2][1]-2) = max(-2, 3-2) = 1

Max is max(dp[3][1], dp[3][-1]) = max(3,1) = 3. That matches the expected 3. Good.

Now after swap (1,2): array becomes [3,1,2]. Recompute DP from scratch.
i=1, a[1]=3: dp[1][1]=3, dp[1][-1]=-inf.
i=2, a[2]=1: dp[2][1]=max(3, -inf+1, 1)=3, dp[2][-1]=max(-inf, 3-1)=2.
i=3, a[3]=2: dp[3][1]=max(3, 2+2, 2)=max(3,4,2)=4, dp[3][-1]=max(2, 3-2)=2.
Max is 4. Expected output after swap is 4. Matches.

So the DP works for this small case. Now let me check the second test case: n=2, q=2, a=[1,2], swaps (1,2) then (1,2). Initial: [1,2].
i=1: dp[1][1]=1, dp[1][-1]=-inf.
i=2: dp[2][1]=max(1, -inf+2, 2)=2, dp[2][-1]=max(-inf, 1-2)=-1. Max=2. Expected: 2.
After first swap: [2,1].
i=1: dp[1][1]=2, dp[1][-1]=-inf.
i=2: dp[2][1]=max(2, -inf+1, 1)=2, dp[2][-1]=max(-inf, 2-1)=1. Max=2. Expected: 2.
After second swap: back to [1,2], max=2. Expected: 2. All match.

Third test case: n=7, q=5, a=[1,2,5,4,3,6,7]. Let me trace initial DP fully.
i=1, a=1: dp[1][1]=1, dp[1][-1]=-inf.
i=2, a=2: dp[2][1]=max(1, -inf+2, 2)=2, dp[2][-1]=max(-inf, 1-2)=-1.
i=3, a=5: dp[3][1]=max(2, -1+5, 5)=max(2,4,5)=5, dp[3][-1]=max(-1, 2-5)=max(-1,-3)=-1.
i=4, a=4: dp[4][1]=max(5, -1+4, 4)=max(5,3,4)=5, dp[4][-1]=max(-1, 5-4)=1.
i=5, a=3: dp[5][1]=max(5, 1+3, 3)=max(5,4,3)=5, dp[5][-1]=max(1, 5-3)=2.
i=6, a=6: dp[6][1]=max(5, 2+6, 6)=max(5,8,6)=8, dp[6][-1]=max(2, 5-6)=max(2,-1)=2.
i=7, a=7: dp[7][1]=max(8, 2+7, 7)=max(8,9,7)=9, dp[7][-1]=max(2, 8-7)=max(2,1)=2.
Max=9. Expected initial output: 9. Matches.

Now after each swap, I'd need to recompute the entire DP from scratch. For n=7, that's 7 steps, trivial. But constraint says sum of n over all test cases ≤ 3×10^5, and sum of q ≤ 3×10^5. If I recompute DP from scratch after each swap, the total work is O((q+1) × n) per test case. In the worst case, one test case could have n=3×10^5 and q=3×10^5. Then total operations = (3×10^5 + 1) × 3×10^5 ≈ 9×10^10. Budget is 2×10^8. That's 9×10^10 / 2×10^8 = 450× over budget.

wait — (q+1)×n = 9×10^10, which is 450× over the 2×10^8 budget. Full recomputation after each swap is way too slow.

LEARNING: Full DP recomputation after each swap costs O(q×n)=9×10^10 ops, 450× over the 2×10^8 budget.

I'll build a segment tree where each node stores four values: the best alternating sum for a subsequence starting with a positive term (+start), starting with a negative term (-start), ending with a positive term (+end), and ending with a negative term (-end). Actually, for merging two adjacent segments, I need to know the best alternating sum that can be formed from the combined range, respecting the alternating sign pattern. Let me think about what each node should store.

A subsequence from a range has a sign pattern: + - + - ... or - + - + ... depending on whether the first chosen element is added or subtracted. But the army strength is always a_{b_1} - a_{b_2} + a_{b_3} - ... so the first term is always positive. So I only care about subsequences that start with a positive term. However, when merging left and right children, a subsequence could span both: it might take some elements from the left child (ending with some sign) and continue into the right child (starting with the opposite sign). So each node should store:

- best_pos_start: maximum alternating sum of a subsequence within this segment that starts with a positive term (i.e., first element added).
- best_neg_start: maximum alternating sum starting with a negative term (first element subtracted). But wait — the overall army always starts positive. The neg_start is only useful as a suffix of a larger subsequence. Actually, I think the standard approach for this problem stores four values per node: the maximum alternating sum of a subsequence that starts with + and ends with +, starts with + and ends with -, starts with - and ends with +, starts with - and ends with -. Let me denote them as pp, pm, mp, mm where first letter is start sign, second is end sign.

For a leaf node with value x:
- pp = x (take just this element, start +, end +)
- pm = -∞ (can't start + and end - with one element)
- mp = -∞ (can't start - and end + with one element)
- mm = -x (take just this element but treat start as -, so value is -x, end is -)

When merging left node L and right node R:
- pp = max(L.pp, R.pp, L.pp + R.mp, L.pm + R.pp)? Let me reason carefully. A subsequence in the merged range that starts + and ends + can be: entirely in L (L.pp), entirely in R (R.pp), or spans both. If it spans both, the left part starts + and ends with some sign, and the right part starts with the opposite sign and ends +. If left part ends +, right part must start - and end +: L.pp + R.mp. If left part ends -, right part must start + and end +: L.pm + R.pp. So pp = max(L.pp, R.pp, L.pp + R.mp, L.pm + R.pp).

- pm = max(L.pm, R.pm, L.pp + R.mm, L.pm + R.pm). Left ends + means right starts - and ends -: L.pp + R.mm. Left ends - means right starts + and ends -: L.pm + R.pm.

- mp = max(L.mp, R.mp, L.mp + R.mp, L.mm + R.pp). Wait: start - end +. Entirely L: L.mp. Entirely R: R.mp. Span: left ends + so right starts - and ends +: L.mp? No, if left starts - and ends +, right must start - and end +: L.mp + R.mp? That would be - + - + which is start - end +, yes. If left starts - and ends -, right starts + and ends +: L.mm + R.pp. So mp = max(L.mp, R.mp, L.mp + R.mp, L.mm + R.pp).

- mm = max(L.mm, R.mm, L.mp + R.mm, L.mm + R.pm). Start - end -. Entirely L: L.mm. Entirely R: R.mm. Span: left ends + so right starts - and ends -: L.mp + R.mm. Left ends - so right starts + and ends -: L.mm + R.pm.

The answer for the whole array is pp of the root, since the army must start with a positive term and can end with either sign (but pp is start + end +, and pm is start + end -). Actually the army can end with either sign. The maximum strength is max(root.pp, root.pm). Because the subsequence starts + and ends either + or -.

Now let me trace Example 1. First test case: n=3, q=1, a=[1,3,2], swap (1,2). Initial array: [1,3,2].

Build leaves:
Leaf 1 (value 1): pp=1, pm=-∞, mp=-∞, mm=-1
Leaf 2 (value 3): pp=3, pm=-∞, mp=-∞, mm=-3
Leaf 3 (value 2): pp=2, pm=-∞, mp=-∞, mm=-2

pm = max(-∞, -∞, 1+(-3), -∞+(-∞)) = max(-∞,-∞,-2,-∞) = -2

mm = max(-1, -2, 2+(-∞), -1+(-∞)) = max(-1,-2,-∞,-∞) = -1

mm = max(-1,-2,-2+(-∞),-1+(-∞)) = max(-1,-2,-∞,-∞) = -1

Now second test case: n=2, q=2, a=[1,2], swaps (1,2) and (1,2). Initial: [1,2].
Leaf 1: pp=1, pm=-∞, mp=-∞, mm=-1
Leaf 2: pp=2, pm=-∞, mp=-∞, mm=-2

Third test case: n=7, q=5, a=[1,2,5,4,3,6,7]. Let me trace initial array quickly. I'll build the tree.

Merge [3,4]: pp=max(5,4,-∞,-∞)=5, pm=max(-∞,-∞,5+(-4),-∞)=1, mp=max(-∞,-∞,-∞,-5+4)=-1, mm=max(-5,-4,-∞,-∞)=-4

Merge [1,2] and [3,4] into [1,4]:

R: pp=5, pm=1, mp=-1, mm=-4

<code>
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

vll ans;

void solve() {
    int n, q;
    cin >> n >> q;
    n++;
    vll s(n + 1);
    s[0] = 0;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n - 1; ++i) cin >> s[i];
    s[n] = 0;
    vector<int> localmax(n, 0), localmin(n, 0);
    ll maxs = 0, mins = 0;
    auto ok = [&](int ind) -> bool {
        return ind >= 1 && ind <= n - 1;
    };
    auto check = [&](const vector<int>& inds) {
        for (int ind : inds) {
            if (!ok(ind)) continue;
            if (localmax[ind] == 0 && s[ind - 1] < s[ind] && s[ind] > s[ind + 1]) {
                localmax[ind] = 1;
                maxs += s[ind];
            }
            if (localmin[ind] == 0 && s[ind - 1] > s[ind] && s[ind] < s[ind + 1]) {
                localmin[ind] = 1;
                mins += s[ind];
            }
        }
    };
    auto upd0 = [&](const vector<int>& inds) {
        for (int ind : inds) {
            if (!ok(ind)) continue;
            if (localmax[ind]) {
                localmax[ind] = 0;
                maxs -= s[ind];
            }
            if (localmin[ind]) {
                localmin[ind] = 0;
                mins -= s[ind];
            }
        }
    };
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) check({i});
    ans.pb(maxs - mins);
    while (q--) {
        int l, r;
        cin >> l >> r;
        upd0({l, l - 1, l + 1, r, r - 1, r + 1});
        swap(s[l], s[r]);
        check({l, l - 1, l + 1, r, r - 1, r + 1});
        ans.pb(maxs - mins);
    }
}

int main() {
    fastio;
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
    for (auto x : ans) cout << x << '\n';
    return 0;
}
</code>

Okay so we have an array a of n distinct integers, and we need to find the maximum alternating sum of any non-empty subsequence. The subsequence must preserve order. The alternating sum is a_{b1} - a_{b2} + a_{b3} - a_{b4} + ... . We also have q swap operations, and after each swap we need to output the new maximum. The constraints: sum of n over all test cases ≤ 3×10^5, sum of q ≤ 3×10^5. Time limit 2 seconds, so budget roughly 2×10^8 operations.

I'll try the DP approach with sign tracking. Define dp[i][1] as the maximum alternating sum achievable using elements from the first i positions, where the last taken element was added with a plus sign. dp[i][-1] is the same but the last taken element was subtracted. The recurrence: dp[i][1] = max(dp[i-1][1], dp[i-1][-1] + a[i]), and dp[i][-1] = max(dp[i-1][-1], dp[i-1][1] - a[i]). Base: dp[0][1] = -infinity (or 0 if we allow empty? but army must be non-empty, so we need to handle first pick carefully). Actually, we can start with dp[0][1] = 0 meaning we haven't picked anything yet and the next pick will be positive, and dp[0][-1] = -infinity. Then at each step we can either skip a[i] or take it with the appropriate sign.

Let me trace Example 1, first test case: n=3, q=1, a=[1,3,2], swap (1,2). Initial array: [1,3,2].
Initialize: dp[0][1]=0, dp[0][-1]=-inf.
i=1, a[1]=1:
  dp[1][1] = max(dp[0][1], dp[0][-1]+1) = max(0, -inf+1) = 0
  dp[1][-1] = max(dp[0][-1], dp[0][1]-1) = max(-inf, 0-1) = -1
i=2, a[2]=3:
  dp[2][1] = max(dp[1][1], dp[1][-1]+3) = max(0, -1+3) = max(0,2) = 2
  dp[2][-1] = max(dp[1][-1], dp[1][1]-3) = max(-1, 0-3) = -1
i=3, a[3]=2:

  dp[3][-1] = max(dp[2][-1], dp[2][1]-2) = max(-1, 2-2) = max(-1,0) = 0

The maximum alternating sum is max(dp[3][1], dp[3][-1]) = max(2,0) = 2. But the expected output for initial array is 3. The subsequence [3] gives 3, which is better. My DP gave 2. Let me check: dp[2][1]=2 came from taking a[1]=1 with minus? Wait, dp[1][-1]=-1 means we took a[1] with minus sign, so the sequence so far is just [-1]? That doesn't make sense — the first element should be positive. The recurrence allows taking a[i] with minus even if nothing was taken before, because dp[i-1][1] could be 0 (empty sequence) and then dp[i][-1] = 0 - a[i] = -a[i]. That's wrong — the first element must be positive. So the DP state needs to track whether we've taken at least one element. Let me adjust: dp[i][0] = max sum ending with no element taken (always 0), dp[i][1] = max sum ending with a positive pick, dp[i][-1] = max sum ending with a negative pick. Then dp[i][1] = max(dp[i-1][1], dp[i-1][-1] + a[i]), but also we can start a new sequence: dp[i][1] = max(dp[i][1], a[i]). And dp[i][-1] = max(dp[i-1][-1], dp[i-1][1] - a[i]), but we cannot start with a negative, so no standalone -a[i].

Let me re-trace with this fix. a=[1,3,2].
dp[0][0]=0, dp[0][1]=-inf, dp[0][-1]=-inf.
i=1, a[1]=1:
  dp[1][1] = max(dp[0][1], dp[0][-1]+1, 1) = max(-inf, -inf+1, 1) = 1
  dp[1][-1] = max(dp[0][-1], dp[0][1]-1) = max(-inf, -inf-1) = -inf (can't start with minus)
i=2, a[2]=3:
  dp[2][1] = max(dp[1][1], dp[1][-1]+3, 3) = max(1, -inf+3, 3) = 3
  dp[2][-1] = max(dp[1][-1], dp[1][1]-3) = max(-inf, 1-3) = -2
i=3, a[3]=2:

  dp[3][-1] = max(dp[2][-1], dp[2][1]-2) = max(-2, 3-2) = 1

Max is max(dp[3][1], dp[3][-1]) = max(3,1) = 3. That matches the expected 3. Good.

Now after each swap, I'd need to recompute the entire DP from scratch. For n=7, that's 7 steps, trivial. But constraint says sum of n over all test cases ≤ 3×10^5, and sum of q ≤ 3×10^5. If I recompute DP from scratch after each swap, the total work is O((q+1) × n) per test case. In the worst case, one test case could have n=3×10^5 and q=3×10^5. Then total operations = (3×10^5 + 1) × 3×10^5 ≈ 9×10^10. Budget is 2×10^8. That's 9×10^10 / 2×10^8 = 450× over budget.

finetuned.full: To solve this problem, we need to determine the maximum alternating sum (maximal strength) of any subsequence of the given array after performing up to \( q \) swaps. The key observation here is that the optimal strategy involves always picking the largest possible values for positions where they contribute positively and the smallest possible values for positions where they contribute negatively. Given that the array elements are distinct, sorting them doesn't directly help because swapping adjacent elements might change the contribution pattern significantly. Instead, we need a data structure that allows efficient insertion, deletion, and retrieval of the k-th smallest/largest element. A balanced binary search tree (BST), like a set or multiset in C++, would allow us to insert, delete, and find the k-th smallest/largest element efficiently. However, we need to handle range queries and updates. A segment tree with lazy propagation could be used to maintain the sorted order of elements and answer range queries about the best possible alternating sums. However, the constraint \( n \leq 3 \times 10^5 \) makes a naive segment tree approach too slow due to the high number of operations per test case. We need a more efficient method. We can preprocess the initial array by computing the prefix sums of the differences between consecutive elements. This helps in quickly calculating the alternating sum of any subsequence. After processing the initial array, we can simulate the swaps and update the prefix sums accordingly. For each swap, we need to adjust the prefix sums to account for the new ordering. This can be done using a segment tree with lazy propagation to support point updates and range queries on prefix sums. Here’s a step-by-step outline: 1. **Initial Processing**: - Sort the array \( a \). - Compute the prefix sums of the differences between consecutive elements. This gives us a new array \( p \) where \( p[i] = a[i] - a[i-1] \). 2. **Segment Tree**: - Build a segment tree on the prefix sums array \( p \) to support range queries and updates. 3. **Handling Swaps**: - For each swap, identify the elements involved and compute how their contributions change when swapped. - Update the segment tree to reflect the new contributions. 4. **Querying Maximum Alternating Sum**: - To get the maximum alternating sum, consider all subsequences and calculate the sum based on the current ordering of elements. - Since the elements are distinct, we can use the prefix sums to compute the sum of any subsequence efficiently. Let’s implement this outline in C++. #include using namespace std; #define ll long long #define pb push_back #define mp make_pair const int MAXN = 3e5 + 10; int n, q; int arr[MAXN]; int orig[MAXN]; struct Node { ll val; int pos; }; bool cmp(Node &a, Node &b) { return a.val > b.val; } vector nodes; void init(int idx, int tl, int tr, vector &p, vector &seg, vector &lazy) { seg[idx].val = p[tl]; seg[idx].pos = tl; if (tl == tr) return; int tm = (tl + tr) / 2; init(idx * 2, tl, tm, p, seg, lazy); init(idx * 2 + 1, tm + 1, tr, p, seg, lazy); } Node query(int idx, int tl, int tr, int l, int r, vector &seg, vector &lazy) { if (lazy[idx]) { seg[idx].val *= -1; if (tl != tr) { lazy[idx * 2] = !lazy[idx * 2]; lazy[idx * 2 + 1] = !lazy[idx * 2 + 1]; } lazy[idx] = false; } if (l > tr || r < tl) return {0, 0}; if (l <= tl && tr <= r) return seg[idx]; int tm = (tl + tr) / 2; Node left = query(idx * 2, tl, tm, l, r, seg, lazy); Node right = query(idx * 2 + 1, tm + 1, tr, l, r, seg, lazy); if (left.val > right.val) return left; return right; } void update(int idx, int tl, int tr, int pos, ll val, vector &seg, vector &lazy) { if (lazy[idx]) { seg[idx].val *= -1; if (tl != tr) { lazy[idx * 2] = !lazy[idx * 2]; lazy[idx * 2 + 1] = !lazy[idx * 2 + 1]; } lazy[idx] = false; } if (tl == tr) { seg[idx].val = val; seg[idx].pos = pos; return; } int tm = (tl + tr) / 2; if (pos <= tm) update(idx * 2, tl, tm, pos, val, seg, lazy); else update(idx * 2 + 1, tm + 1, tr, pos, val, seg, lazy); if (seg[idx * 2].val > seg[idx * 2 + 1].val) { seg[idx].val = seg[idx * 2].val; seg[idx].pos = seg[idx * 2].pos; } else { seg[idx].val = seg[idx * 2 + 1].val; seg[idx].pos = seg[idx * 2 + 1].pos; } } ll solve(vector &p) { vector seg(4 * n); vector lazy(4 * n, false); init(1, 0, n - 1, p, seg, lazy); ll ans = 0; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { Node res = query(1, 0, n - 1, i, n - 1, seg, lazy); ans += res.val; } return ans; } void process() { cin >> n >> q; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { cin >> arr[i]; orig[i] = arr[i]; } sort(arr, arr + n); vector p(n); p[0] = arr[0]; for (int i = 1; i < n; ++i) p[i] = arr[i] - arr[i - 1]; nodes.clear(); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) nodes.pb({p[i], i}); sort(nodes.begin(), nodes.end(), cmp); vector mapping(n); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) mapping[nodes[i].pos] = i; vector new_order(n); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) new_order[mapping[i]] = i; ll cur_ans = solve(p); cout << cur_ans << ' '; while (q--) { int l, r; cin >> l >> r; --l, --r; int old_l = orig[l], old_r = orig[r]; orig[l] = old_r; orig[r] = old_l; int pos_l = mapping[l]; int pos_r = mapping[r]; int new_pos_l = mapping[pos_l]; int new_pos_r = mapping[pos_r]; mapping[l] = new_pos_r; mapping[r] = new_pos_l; mapping[new_pos_l] = l; mapping[new_pos_r] = r; swap(new_order[new_pos_l], new_order[new_pos_r]); int val_l = new_order[new_pos_l]; int val_r = new_order[new_pos_r]; int old_p_l = pos_l - val_l; int old_p_r = pos_r - val_r; int new_p_l = new_pos_r - val_l; int new_p_r = new_pos_l - val_r; int delta_l = new_p_l - old_p_l; int delta_r = new_p_r - old_p_r; p[val_l] -= delta_l; p[val_r] += delta_r; ll next_ans = solve(p); cout << next_ans << ' '; } cout << '\n'; } int main() { ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); int t; cin >> t; while (t--) process(); return 0; }

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
In a far away galaxy there are n inhabited planets, numbered with numbers from 1 to n. They are located at large distances from each other, that's why the communication between them was very difficult until on the planet number 1 a hyperdrive was invented. As soon as this significant event took place, n - 1 spaceships were built on the planet number 1, and those ships were sent to other planets to inform about the revolutionary invention.

Paradoxical thought it may be, but the hyperspace is represented as simple three-dimensional Euclidean space. The inhabited planets may be considered fixed points in it, and no two points coincide and no three points lie on the same straight line. The movement of a ship with a hyperdrive between two planets is performed along a straight line at the constant speed, the same for all the ships. That's why the distance in the hyperspace are measured in hyperyears (a ship with a hyperdrive covers a distance of s hyperyears in s years).

When the ship reaches an inhabited planet, the inhabitants of the planet dissemble it, make n - 2 identical to it ships with a hyperdrive and send them to other n - 2 planets (except for the one from which the ship arrived). The time to make a new ship compared to the time in which they move from one planet to another is so small that it can be disregarded. New ships are absolutely identical to the ones sent initially: they move at the same constant speed along a straight line trajectory and, having reached a planet, perform the very same mission, i.e. are dissembled to build new n - 2 ships and send them to all the planets except for the one from which the ship arrived. Thus, the process of spreading the important news around the galaxy continues.

However the hyperdrive creators hurried to spread the news about their invention so much that they didn't study completely what goes on when two ships collide in the hyperspace. If two moving ships find themselves at one point, they provoke an explosion of colossal power, leading to the destruction of the galaxy!

Your task is to find the time the galaxy will continue to exist from the moment of the ships' launch from the first planet.

Time limit: 2s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
The first line contains a number n (3 ≤ n ≤ 5000) — the number of inhabited planets in the galaxy. The next n lines contain integer coordinates of the planets in format "xi yi zi" ( - 104 ≤ xi, yi, zi ≤ 104).

Output:
Print the single number — the solution to the task with an absolute or relative error not exceeding 10 - 6.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    4
    0 0 0
    0 0 1
    0 1 0
    1 0 0
    Output:
    1.7071067812

reference.full: Okay so we have n planets as points in 3D, no two coincide, no three collinear. From planet 1, n-1 ships launch to all other planets at time 0. When a ship arrives at planet P from planet Q at time t, it spawns n-2 new ships from P to all planets except Q, starting at time t. All ships move at constant speed along straight lines. We need the earliest time two ships collide at the same point in space. The constraint: n ≤ 5000. Time limit 2.0s. So we're looking at O(n²) or O(n² log n) maybe, but not O(n³). Let me try the direct pairwise collision simulation. I'll maintain a list of all ship trajectories. Each ship is defined by its start planet A, destination planet B, and start time t0. Its position at time τ ≥ t0 is: A + ((τ - t0) / d(A,B)) * (B - A), where d is Euclidean distance. Speed is 1 unit per hyperyear. Two ships can collide only if their trajectories intersect at some point in 3D, and they both reach that point at the same time. For two ships with start times t1, t2, from A1→B1 and A2→B2, I need to solve for τ such that both ships are at the same position at time τ. That means finding if there exist parameters α, β in [0,1] and a time τ ≥ max(t1,t2) such that: A1 + α*(B1-A1) = A2 + β*(B2-A2), and τ = t1 + α*d1 = t2 + β*d2, where d1 = d(A1,B1), d2 = d(A2,B2). This is a system of 4 equations (3 spatial + 1 time) with 3 unknowns (α, β, τ). So it's overdetermined — most pairs won't collide. I'd need to solve for α, β from the spatial equations, then check if the time equation holds and if τ ≥ both start times. But the real problem is the number of ships. Let me trace Example 1 first. Example 1: n=4. Planets: 1=(0,0,0), 2=(0,0,1), 3=(0,1,0), 4=(1,0,0). Initial ships from planet 1 at t=0: ship to 2, ship to 3, ship to 4. That's 3 ships. Distances: d(1,2)=1, d(1,3)=1, d(1,4)=1. So all three arrive at t=1. At t=1, ship arrives at planet 2 from planet 1. It spawns n-2 = 2 new ships: from 2 to 3, and from 2 to 4. Start time t=1. Simultaneously at t=1, ship arrives at planet 3 from planet 1. Spawns 2 ships: 3→2, 3→4. Start time t=1. And at planet 4: spawns 2 ships: 4→2, 4→3. Start time t=1. So now we have the original 3 ships (already arrived, done), plus 6 new ships starting at t=1. Total ships so far: 9. Now these 6 ships travel. Distances: d(2,3)=√2≈1.414, d(2,4)=√2, d(3,4)=√2. They all arrive at t=1+√2≈2.414. When a ship arrives at a planet, it spawns n-2=2 more ships, except back to where it came from. So at t=2.414, each of the 6 arrivals spawns 2 new ships. That's 12 new ships. But wait — some of these might be heading to planets that already have ships en route? Doesn't matter, they all launch. And this process continues. Each arrival at a planet spawns n-2 new ships. The number of ships grows exponentially. At each "generation," every ship that arrives spawns n-2 new ones. The first generation: n-1 ships. Second generation: (n-1)*(n-2) ships. Third generation: (n-1)*(n-2)*(n-2) ships? Actually, each arrival spawns n-2, and there are (n-1) arrivals in first gen, so second gen has (n-1)*(n-2) ships. Each of those arrives and spawns n-2, so third gen has (n-1)*(n-2)^2 ships. In general, generation k has (n-1)*(n-2)^{k-1} ships. For n=5000, n-2=4998. Even just generation 2 has (4999)*(4998) ≈ 25 million ships. Generation 3 would have ~125 billion. There's no upper bound on generations — ships keep bouncing around forever, spawning more. The number of ships is infinite over time. So I can't enumerate all ships, let alone check all pairs. Even if I only consider ships active at any moment, the number of simultaneous active ships grows without bound as time increases, because ships keep getting spawned and traveling. wait — the number of ships grows unboundedly over time. Generation 2 alone is ~25 million ships for n=5000, and checking all pairs among them is O(m²) where m is number of active ships, which is already ~3×10^14 pairs just for gen 2, completely impossible. LEARNING: Ship count grows as (n-1)*(n-2)^{k-1} per generation k; for n=5000, generation 2 has ~2.5×10^7 ships, making pairwise collision checking O(m²) infeasible. I'll simulate the ships as moving points and check for the earliest collision time among all pairs. Each ship moves along a straight line from its source planet to its target planet at speed 1 (distance in hyperyears equals time in years). So a ship from planet A to planet B has parametric position A + t*(B-A)/d, where d = distance(A,B), for t from 0 to d. Two ships (from A1→B1 and A2→B2) collide if there exists a time t where their positions are equal. That's a vector equation: A1 + t*(B1-A1)/d1 = A2 + t*(B2-A2)/d2. This is three scalar equations linear in t, but the two ships may have different start times — wait, all ships from the same generation start at the same time? Actually, ships are launched immediately when a ship arrives at a planet. So each ship has its own start time equal to the arrival time of the ship that brought the news to its source planet. That means each ship's motion is defined by a start time s, source planet src, target planet dst, distance d = dist(src,dst), and its position for global time T is: if T < s then not yet started; if s ≤ T ≤ s+d then at src + (T-s)*(dst-src)/d; if T > s+d then already arrived and gone. So to find the earliest collision, I need to consider all pairs of ships that are moving simultaneously, solve for T such that their positions match, and ensure T is within both ships' active intervals. But the number of ships grows exponentially: generation 1: n-1 ships from planet 1 to all others. Generation 2: each of those n-1 planets sends n-2 ships to all planets except the one it came from. So total ships after generation 2 is (n-1)*(n-2). For n=5000, that's 4999*4998 ≈ 2.5×10^7 ships just from the first two generations. Pairwise checking among these 2.5×10^7 ships would be about (2.5e7)²/2 ≈ 3×10^14 pairs, which is astronomically too many. Because ships are only built when a ship arrives at a planet, and they are sent to all other planets except the one they came from. So consider any pair of planets A and B. The first time a ship can travel from A to B is when a ship first arrives at A from some other planet C (C ≠ B). That ship gets dismantled and sends a new ship from A to B. Similarly, a ship traveling from B to A can only be launched from B after a ship first arrives there from some other planet D (D ≠ A). So the paths A→B and B→A are activated at specific times: the arrival times at A and B respectively. A collision on the segment between A and B happens if both directions are active and the ships meet in the middle. Since they travel at the same speed, they meet exactly halfway if they start at the same time. But if they start at different times, the meeting point shifts. Let me formalize. Let t[X] be the earliest time a ship arrives at planet X. For planet 1, t[1] = 0. For any other planet, its first arrival time is the minimum over all other planets Y of (t[Y] + dist(Y, X)). Because the first ship to reach X must come from some Y that already has ships. Once t[X] is known, ships are launched from X to all other planets Z (Z ≠ the planet it came from, but that detail might not matter for the *first* arrival at Z, since the ship from X could be the first to reach Z). Actually, for computing the first arrival time at Z, the ship from X is a candidate, arriving at time t[X] + dist(X, Z). So t[Z] = min over all X ≠ Z of (t[X] + dist(X, Z)). That's just Dijkstra's algorithm on a complete graph with edge weights dist(X, Y)! The earliest time the news reaches each planet is the shortest path distance from planet 1 in this metric space. But that's only the arrival times. The galaxy ends when two ships collide. A collision between ships on path X→Y and Y→X happens if both directions are active simultaneously and the ships' paths overlap in time. When is the path X→Y active? A ship is launched from X to Y at time t[X] (the first arrival at X). It travels for dist(X, Y) time, so it's on the segment from time t[X] to t[X] + dist(X, Y). Similarly, a ship from Y to X is on the segment from t[Y] to t[Y] + dist(Y, X). They collide if these time intervals overlap on the physical segment. More precisely, they meet at some point P along the line if the sum of the distances from P to X and P to Y equals dist(X, Y), and the times when each ship reaches P are equal. Let d = dist(X, Y). Ship from X reaches point at distance a from X at time t[X] + a. Ship from Y reaches point at distance d - a from Y at time t[Y] + (d - a). Setting these equal: t[X] + a = t[Y] + d - a → 2a = t[Y] - t[X] + d → a = (t[Y] - t[X] + d) / 2. For a collision to physically happen on the open segment (0 < a < d), we need 0 < a < d. Also, the meeting time must be within both ships' journeys: t[X] ≤ t[X] + a ≤ t[X] + d and t[Y] ≤ t[Y] + (d - a) ≤ t[Y] + d, which hold if a is between 0 and d. So the collision time is T_collision = t[X] + a = t[X] + (t[Y] - t[X] + d)/2 = (t[X] + t[Y] + d) / 2. Therefore, for each unordered pair (X, Y), we can compute a potential collision time T_XY = (t[X] + t[Y] + dist(X, Y)) / 2, provided that the ship from X leaves after t[X] and the ship from Y leaves after t[Y] — which they always do — and that the meeting point is strictly between them (0 < a < d). That condition a > 0 and a < d simplifies to |t[Y] - t[X]| < d. If |t[Y] - t[X]| >= d, then one ship would finish its journey before the other even enters the segment, so no collision. So the galaxy's end time is the minimum T_XY over all pairs (X, Y) satisfying |t[Y] - t[X]| < dist(X, Y). Now I need t[X] for all X. Distances from 1: to 2:1, to 3:1, to 4:1. So t[2]=1, t[3]=1, t[4]=1. Now all t's are known: t = [0, 1, 1, 1]. Now check each pair for collision condition |t[i]-t[j]| < d_ij. Pair (1,2): |0-1|=1, d12=1. Condition 1 < 1? False (strict inequality). So no collision. Pair (1,3): same, |0-1|=1, d13=1 → false. Pair (1,4): same, false. Pair (2,3): |1-1|=0, d23≈1.414. 0 < 1.414 → true. T = (1+1+1.414)/2 = (3.414)/2 ≈ 1.70710678. Pair (2,4): same, |1-1|=0 < 1.414 → true. T ≈ 1.70710678. Pair (3,4): same, T ≈ 1.70710678. Minimum T over all valid pairs is ≈ 1.70710678. That matches the example output. aha! — so the explosion time isn't about the first arrival at some planet, but the first moment two ships cross paths. And because the propagation of ships follows a shortest-path tree in arrival times, the only pairs that can collide are those where the arrival times are close enough relative to the distance between them. The problem reduces to computing all-pairs shortest paths from planet 1 (for arrival times) and then taking the minimum of (t[i]+t[j]+dist(i,j))/2 over pairs where |t[i]-t[j]| < dist(i,j). That's O(n²), perfectly feasible for n=5000. #include using namespace std; #define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); #define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end() #define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend() #define sz(x) (int)(x).size() #define ll long long #define pb push_back #define pob pop_back #define vll vector #define pll pair #define vpll vector #define endl '\n' #define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a)) #define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a)) ll gcd(ll a, ll b) { while (a > 0) { ll temp = b % a; b = a; a = temp; } return b; } ll lcm(ll a, ll b) { return a / gcd(a, b) * b; } ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) { ll t = n / k; if (n % k == 0) return t; return t + 1; } ll pw(ll a, ll b) { ll r = 1; while (b) { if (b & 1) r *= a; a *= a; b >>= 1; } return r; } ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) { ll r = 1 % mod; a %= mod; while (b) { if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod; a = a * a % mod; b >>= 1; } return r; } // helper: squared distance between two planets ll dist2(const vll& x, const vll& y, const vll& z, int i, int j) { ll dx = x[i] - x[j]; ll dy = y[i] - y[j]; ll dz = z[i] - z[j]; return dx * dx + dy * dy + dz * dz; } void solve() { int n; cin >> n; vll x(n), y(n), z(n); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { cin >> x[i] >> y[i] >> z[i]; } const double INF = 1e18; vector t(n, INF); vector vis(n, false); t[0] = 0.0; for (int iter = 0; iter < n; ++iter) { int u = -1; double best = INF; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { if (!vis[i] && t[i] < best) { best = t[i]; u = i; } } if (u == -1) break; vis[u] = true; for (int v = 0; v < n; ++v) { if (!vis[v]) { double d = sqrt((double)dist2(x, y, z, u, v)); if (t[u] + d < t[v]) { t[v] = t[u] + d; } } } } double ans = INF; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { for (int j = i + 1; j < n; ++j) { double d = sqrt((double)dist2(x, y, z, i, j)); if (fabs(t[i] - t[j]) < d - 1e-12) { double cand = (t[i] + t[j] + d) / 2.0; if (cand < ans) ans = cand; } } } cout << ans << endl; } int main() { fastio; cout << fixed << setprecision(10); solve(); return 0; }

Okay so we have n planets as points in 3D, no two coincide, no three collinear. From planet 1, n-1 ships launch to all other planets at time 0. When a ship arrives at planet P from planet Q at time t, it spawns n-2 new ships from P to all planets except Q, starting at time t. All ships move at constant speed along straight lines. We need the earliest time two ships collide at the same point in space.

The constraint: n ≤ 5000. Time limit 2.0s. So we're looking at O(n²) or O(n² log n) maybe, but not O(n³).

Let me try the direct pairwise collision simulation. I'll maintain a list of all ship trajectories. Each ship is defined by its start planet A, destination planet B, and start time t0. Its position at time τ ≥ t0 is: A + ((τ - t0) / d(A,B)) * (B - A), where d is Euclidean distance. Speed is 1 unit per hyperyear.

Two ships can collide only if their trajectories intersect at some point in 3D, and they both reach that point at the same time. For two ships with start times t1, t2, from A1→B1 and A2→B2, I need to solve for τ such that both ships are at the same position at time τ. That means finding if there exist parameters α, β in [0,1] and a time τ ≥ max(t1,t2) such that:

A1 + α*(B1-A1) = A2 + β*(B2-A2), and τ = t1 + α*d1 = t2 + β*d2, where d1 = d(A1,B1), d2 = d(A2,B2).

This is a system of 4 equations (3 spatial + 1 time) with 3 unknowns (α, β, τ). So it's overdetermined — most pairs won't collide. I'd need to solve for α, β from the spatial equations, then check if the time equation holds and if τ ≥ both start times.

But the real problem is the number of ships. Let me trace Example 1 first.

Example 1: n=4. Planets: 1=(0,0,0), 2=(0,0,1), 3=(0,1,0), 4=(1,0,0).

Initial ships from planet 1 at t=0: ship to 2, ship to 3, ship to 4. That's 3 ships.

Distances: d(1,2)=1, d(1,3)=1, d(1,4)=1. So all three arrive at t=1.

At t=1, ship arrives at planet 2 from planet 1. It spawns n-2 = 2 new ships: from 2 to 3, and from 2 to 4. Start time t=1.

Simultaneously at t=1, ship arrives at planet 3 from planet 1. Spawns 2 ships: 3→2, 3→4. Start time t=1.

And at planet 4: spawns 2 ships: 4→2, 4→3. Start time t=1.

So now we have the original 3 ships (already arrived, done), plus 6 new ships starting at t=1. Total ships so far: 9.

Now these 6 ships travel. Distances: d(2,3)=√2≈1.414, d(2,4)=√2, d(3,4)=√2. They all arrive at t=1+√2≈2.414.

When a ship arrives at a planet, it spawns n-2=2 more ships, except back to where it came from. So at t=2.414, each of the 6 arrivals spawns 2 new ships. That's 12 new ships. But wait — some of these might be heading to planets that already have ships en route? Doesn't matter, they all launch.

And this process continues. Each arrival at a planet spawns n-2 new ships. The number of ships grows exponentially. At each "generation," every ship that arrives spawns n-2 new ones. The first generation: n-1 ships. Second generation: (n-1)*(n-2) ships. Third generation: (n-1)*(n-2)*(n-2) ships? Actually, each arrival spawns n-2, and there are (n-1) arrivals in first gen, so second gen has (n-1)*(n-2) ships. Each of those arrives and spawns n-2, so third gen has (n-1)*(n-2)^2 ships. In general, generation k has (n-1)*(n-2)^{k-1} ships.

For n=5000, n-2=4998. Even just generation 2 has (4999)*(4998) ≈ 25 million ships. Generation 3 would have ~125 billion. There's no upper bound on generations — ships keep bouncing around forever, spawning more. The number of ships is infinite over time.

So I can't enumerate all ships, let alone check all pairs. Even if I only consider ships active at any moment, the number of simultaneous active ships grows without bound as time increases, because ships keep getting spawned and traveling.

A ship is launched from X to Y at time t[X] (the first arrival at X). It travels for dist(X, Y) time, so it's on the segment from time t[X] to t[X] + dist(X, Y). Similarly, a ship from Y to X is on the segment from t[Y] to t[Y] + dist(Y, X). They collide if these time intervals overlap on the physical segment. More precisely, they meet at some point P along the line if the sum of the distances from P to X and P to Y equals dist(X, Y), and the times when each ship reaches P are equal. Let d = dist(X, Y). Ship from X reaches point at distance a from X at time t[X] + a. Ship from Y reaches point at distance d - a from Y at time t[Y] + (d - a). Setting these equal: t[X] + a = t[Y] + d - a → 2a = t[Y] - t[X] + d → a = (t[Y] - t[X] + d) / 2. For a collision to physically happen on the open segment (0 < a < d), we need 0 < a < d. Also, the meeting time must be within both ships' journeys: t[X] ≤ t[X] + a ≤ t[X] + d and t[Y] ≤ t[Y] + (d - a) ≤ t[Y] + d, which hold if a is between 0 and d. So the collision time is T_collision = t[X] + a = t[X] + (t[Y] - t[X] + d)/2 = (t[X] + t[Y] + d) / 2.

Therefore, for each unordered pair (X, Y), we can compute a potential collision time T_XY = (t[X] + t[Y] + dist(X, Y)) / 2, provided that the ship from X leaves after t[X] and the ship from Y leaves after t[Y] — which they always do — and that the meeting point is strictly between them (0 < a < d). That condition a > 0 and a < d simplifies to |t[Y] - t[X]| < d. If |t[Y] - t[X]| >= d, then one ship would finish its journey before the other even enters the segment, so no collision. So the galaxy's end time is the minimum T_XY over all pairs (X, Y) satisfying |t[Y] - t[X]| < dist(X, Y).

Now I need t[X] for all X. That's Dijkstra on a complete graph of n=5000 nodes, which is O(n²) with a naive implementation — that's 25 million operations, fine. Then compute pairwise minima over O(n²) pairs — another 25 million, also fine.

Let me trace on Example 1: n=4, coordinates: P1=(0,0,0), P2=(0,0,1), P3=(0,1,0), P4=(1,0,0). Compute distances: d12 = 1, d13 = 1, d14 = 1, d23 = sqrt(2)≈1.41421356, d24 = sqrt(2)≈1.41421356, d34 = sqrt(2)≈1.41421356.

First, compute t[1]=0. Run Dijkstra:
Start with set {1}. Distances from 1: to 2:1, to 3:1, to 4:1. So t[2]=1, t[3]=1, t[4]=1.
Now all t's are known: t = [0, 1, 1, 1].

Now check each pair for collision condition |t[i]-t[j]| < d_ij.
Pair (1,2): |0-1|=1, d12=1. Condition 1 < 1? False (strict inequality). So no collision.
Pair (1,3): same, |0-1|=1, d13=1 → false.
Pair (1,4): same, false.
Pair (2,3): |1-1|=0, d23≈1.414. 0 < 1.414 → true. T = (1+1+1.414)/2 = (3.414)/2 ≈ 1.70710678.
Pair (2,4): same, |1-1|=0 < 1.414 → true. T ≈ 1.70710678.
Pair (3,4): same, T ≈ 1.70710678.

Minimum T over all valid pairs is ≈ 1.70710678. That matches the example output.

aha! — so the explosion time isn't about the first arrival at some planet, but the first moment two ships cross paths. And because the propagation of ships follows a shortest-path tree in arrival times, the only pairs that can collide are those where the arrival times are close enough relative to the distance between them. The problem reduces to computing all-pairs shortest paths from planet 1 (for arrival times) and then taking the minimum of (t[i]+t[j]+dist(i,j))/2 over pairs where |t[i]-t[j]| < dist(i,j). That's O(n²), perfectly feasible for n=5000.

finetuned.full: To solve this problem, we need to understand the dynamics of the ships and how they spread out over time. Let's break down the key aspects: 1. **Initial Setup**: We have \( n \) planets in 3D space. Initially, \( n-1 \) ships start from planet 1 and travel to other \( n-1 \) planets. 2. **Movement**: Each ship travels along a straight line at constant speed. When a ship reaches a planet, it disassembles and builds \( n-2 \) new ships, sending them to all other planets except the one it came from. 3. **Collision**: Two ships collide if they occupy the same point at the same time. The goal is to find the time until no more ships can be built without causing a collision. ### Key Observations: - Each ship moves along a straight line at constant speed. - When a ship arrives at a planet, it creates \( n-2 \) new ships, each starting from that planet and heading towards all other planets. - The process repeats recursively. ### Approach: 1. **Calculate Initial Distances**: Compute the Euclidean distance between every pair of planets. 2. **Simulate Ship Movement**: Track the positions and times of all ships. Since the speeds are constant, we can simulate the movement by iterating through time steps. 3. **Check for Collisions**: At each step, check if any two ships occupy the same position at the same time. 4. **Stop Condition**: Stop when no more ships can be created without causing a collision. ### Implementation Details: - Use a priority queue (min-heap) to always expand the closest ship first. - Maintain a set of positions occupied by ships to detect collisions efficiently. ### Complexity Analysis: - Time complexity: Each ship moves linearly, and we need to track their positions over time. The number of ships grows exponentially, but since we stop early, the actual number of simulations is limited by the maximum number of non-colliding ships that can be spawned. - Space complexity: Storing positions and times requires O(n^2) space for the initial distances matrix and O(n) for tracking active ships. ### Sample Test Case Walkthrough: For the given example with 4 planets at coordinates (0,0,0), (0,0,1), (0,1,0), and (1,0,0): - Distance calculations yield specific values. - Simulation would show that the ships will reach certain points at specific times, and eventually a collision occurs at some point in time. Given the constraints, this brute-force simulation might seem too expensive, but the exponential growth of ships ensures that the simulation stops early once no more valid placements exist. #include #include #include #include #include #include using namespace std; struct Point { double x, y, z; }; double dist(const Point& p1, const Point& p2) { return sqrt((p1.x - p2.x) * (p1.x - p2.x) + (p1.y - p2.y) * (p1.y - p2.y) + (p1.z - p2.z) * (p1.z - p2.z)); } struct State { int id; double time; Point pos; bool operator<(const State& other) const { return time > other.time; } }; int main() { ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); int n; cin >> n; vector planets(n); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { cin >> planets[i].x >> planets[i].y >> planets[i].z; } vector> d(n, vector(n)); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { for (int j = i + 1; j < n; ++j) { d[i][j] = dist(planets[i], planets[j]); d[j][i] = d[i][j]; } } priority_queue pq; unordered_set occupied; for (int i = 1; i < n; ++i) { double t = d[0][i]; pq.push({i, t, planets[i]}); occupied.insert(to_string(i) + "," + to_string(t)); } while (!pq.empty()) { auto [id, time, pos] = pq.top(); pq.pop(); for (int j = 0; j < n; ++j) { if (j == id) continue; double dt = d[id][j]; double next_time = time + dt; string key = to_string(j) + "," + to_string(next_time); if (occupied.find(key) != occupied.end()) { continue; // Collision detected } occupied.insert(key); pq.push({j, next_time, planets[j]}); } } cout << fixed << setprecision(15); cout << pq.top().time << endl; return 0; }

To solve this problem, we need to understand the dynamics of the ships and how they spread out over time. Let's break down the key aspects:

1. **Initial Setup**: We have \( n \) planets in 3D space. Initially, \( n-1 \) ships start from planet 1 and travel to other \( n-1 \) planets.

2. **Movement**: Each ship travels along a straight line at constant speed. When a ship reaches a planet, it disassembles and builds \( n-2 \) new ships, sending them to all other planets except the one it came from.

3. **Collision**: Two ships collide if they occupy the same point at the same time. The goal is to find the time until no more ships can be built without causing a collision.

### Key Observations:
- Each ship moves along a straight line at constant speed.
- When a ship arrives at a planet, it creates \( n-2 \) new ships, each starting from that planet and heading towards all other planets.
- The process repeats recursively.

### Approach:
1. **Calculate Initial Distances**: Compute the Euclidean distance between every pair of planets.
2. **Simulate Ship Movement**: Track the positions and times of all ships. Since the speeds are constant, we can simulate the movement by iterating through time steps.
3. **Check for Collisions**: At each step, check if any two ships occupy the same position at the same time.
4. **Stop Condition**: Stop when no more ships can be created without causing a collision.

### Implementation Details:
- Use a priority queue (min-heap) to always expand the closest ship first.
- Maintain a set of positions occupied by ships to detect collisions efficiently.

### Complexity Analysis:
- Time complexity: Each ship moves linearly, and we need to track their positions over time. The number of ships grows exponentially, but since we stop early, the actual number of simulations is limited by the maximum number of non-colliding ships that can be spawned.
- Space complexity: Storing positions and times requires O(n^2) space for the initial distances matrix and O(n) for tracking active ships.

### Sample Test Case Walkthrough:
For the given example with 4 planets at coordinates (0,0,0), (0,0,1), (0,1,0), and (1,0,0):
- Distance calculations yield specific values.
- Simulation would show that the ships will reach certain points at specific times, and eventually a collision occurs at some point in time.

Given the constraints, this brute-force simulation might seem too expensive, but the exponential growth of ships ensures that the simulation stops early once no more valid placements exist.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
It's time polar bears Menshykov and Uslada from the zoo of St. Petersburg and elephant Horace from the zoo of Kiev got down to business. In total, there are n tasks for the day and each animal should do each of these tasks. For each task, they have evaluated its difficulty. Also animals decided to do the tasks in order of their difficulty. Unfortunately, some tasks can have the same difficulty, so the order in which one can perform the tasks may vary.

Menshykov, Uslada and Horace ask you to deal with this nuisance and come up with individual plans for each of them. The plan is a sequence describing the order in which an animal should do all the n tasks. Besides, each of them wants to have its own unique plan. Therefore three plans must form three different sequences. You are to find the required plans, or otherwise deliver the sad news to them by stating that it is impossible to come up with three distinct plans for the given tasks.

Time limit: 1s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
The first line contains integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 2000) — the number of tasks. The second line contains n integers h1, h2, ..., hn (1 ≤ hi ≤ 2000), where hi is the difficulty of the i-th task. The larger number hi is, the more difficult the i-th task is.

Output:
In the first line print "YES" (without the quotes), if it is possible to come up with three distinct plans of doing the tasks. Otherwise print in the first line "NO" (without the quotes). If three desired plans do exist, print in the second line n distinct integers that represent the numbers of the tasks in the order they are done according to the first plan. In the third and fourth line print two remaining plans in the same form.

If there are multiple possible answers, you can print any of them.

Note:
In the first sample the difficulty of the tasks sets one limit: tasks 1 and 4 must be done before tasks 2 and 3. That gives the total of four possible sequences of doing tasks : [1, 4, 2, 3], [4, 1, 2, 3], [1, 4, 3, 2], [4, 1, 3, 2]. You can print any three of them in the answer.

In the second sample there are only two sequences of tasks that meet the conditions — [3, 1, 2, 4, 5] and [3, 1, 4, 2, 5]. Consequently, it is impossible to make three distinct sequences of tasks.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    4
    1 3 3 1
    Output:
    YES
    1 4 2 3
    4 1 2 3
    4 1 3 2

  Example 2:
    Input:
    5
    2 4 1 4 8
    Output:
    NO

reference.full: n up to 2000, so O(n²) is fine. Actually, they say "the larger number hi is, the more difficult the i-th task is." So they want to do tasks in increasing difficulty? Or decreasing? Let's check example: Input: 1 3 3 1. They output sequences: 1 4 2 3, 4 1 2 3, 4 1 3 2. Let's map to difficulties: indices: 1→1, 2→3, 3→3, 4→1. Sequence 1: indices 1,4,2,3 → difficulties: 1,1,3,3 — that's non-decreasing (1,1,3,3). Sequence 2: 4,1,2,3 → difficulties: 1,1,3,3 — same order of difficulties but different indices. Sequence 3: 4,1,3,2 → difficulties: 1,1,3,3 — still non-decreasing. So indeed they want tasks sorted by difficulty non-decreasing (since larger hi is more difficult, but they do easier first? Actually, "in order of their difficulty" could be increasing difficulty, so easier tasks first. Yes, the sequences show difficulties non-decreasing. So each plan must be a permutation of indices such that the sequence of h values is non-decreasing. Thus we need to find three distinct permutations of indices where the h values are sorted non-decreasing. Equivalent: sort tasks by h, but if equal h, we can reorder those indices arbitrarily. So the set of valid permutations corresponds to all permutations of indices within each group of equal difficulty. If any difficulty group has size >= 3, we can get at least 3! = 6 permutations just by reordering that group, so we can easily output three distinct plans. But we need to output actual permutations — we can just take the sorted order by h (with some tie-breaking), then swap two indices within equal group to get second permutation, swap another pair for third. That’s straightforward. If no group has size >= 3, maybe we have two groups each of size 2, then total permutations = 2! * 2! = 4, which is >=3, so still possible. Actually, any case where total number of distinct permutations >=3 works. The total number of permutations is product of factorials of group sizes. So if we have at least two groups of size >=2, that product >= 2*2=4 >=3. If we have one group of size 2 and rest size 1, total permutations = 2, which is insufficient. If all size 1, total permutations = 1. So impossible cases: when all groups size 1 (strictly increasing difficulties) or exactly one group of size 2 and rest size 1. But wait, what about group size >=3? That’s fine. So we can decide: if there exists a difficulty with frequency >=3, we can output three permutations by just swapping within that group. Else if there are at least two difficulties with frequency >=2, then also possible. Else impossible. Check example 2: difficulties: 2,4,1,4,8 → frequencies: 1 appears once, 2 once, 4 twice, 8 once. So only one group of size 2 (value 4). So impossible, output NO. Matches. Now we need to construct three permutations. Let's think construction: First, sort indices by h, with ties broken arbitrarily (e.g., by index). That’s plan 1. For plan 2, we need a different permutation with same non-decreasing h order. We can find the first group of size >=2, swap two indices within that group. That yields a different permutation. For plan 3, if that group size >=3, swap another pair (e.g., rotate three indices). If group size =2, we need another group of size >=2 to swap within. So we need to handle cases: Case A: exists group with count >=3. Let that group be at positions i..i+len-1 in sorted list (same h). Plan1: sorted indices. Plan2: swap first two indices of that group. Plan3: swap first and third indices of that group (or rotate). That gives three distinct. Case B: at least two groups with count =2. Let first group at positions i,i+1, second at j,j+1. Plan1: sorted. Plan2: swap i,i+1. Plan3: swap j,j+1. Those are distinct because swaps in different groups. Edge: what if only one group size=2 and rest size 1? Then only 2 permutations exist, so impossible. Implementation: we can pair (h, index), sort by h, then scan for frequencies. Complexity O(n log n). n <=2000 trivial. Let’s test on given example 1: difficulties: 1,3,3,1. Sorted by h: values 1,1,3,3. Indices: suppose we sort by h then index: (1,1),(1,4),(3,2),(3,3). Plan1: 1 4 2 3. Group sizes: first group size 2 (indices 1,4), second group size 2 (2,3). So case B. Plan2: swap first group -> 4 1 2 3. Plan3: swap second group -> 1 4 3 2? Wait, but example output third plan is 4 1 3 2, which is swapping both groups? Actually they swapped both groups relative to plan1? Let's see: plan1: 1 4 2 3. plan2: 4 1 2 3 (swap first group). plan3: 4 1 3 2 (swap first group and second group). That’s also valid. Our method of swapping only second group yields 1 4 3 2, which is distinct from plan1 and plan2. That’s also acceptable, since any three distinct plans work. So we can just swap separate groups. Thus final approach: compute frequencies of h. If impossible as per condition, print NO. Else print YES, then produce three permutations as described. Complexity O(n log n). #include using namespace std; #define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); #define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end() #define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend() #define sz(x) (int)(x).size() #define ll long long #define pb push_back #define pob pop_back #define vll vector #define pll pair #define vpll vector #define endl '\n' #define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a)) #define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a)) ll gcd(ll a, ll b) { while (a > 0) { ll temp = b % a; b = a; a = temp; } return b; } ll lcm(ll a, ll b) { return a / gcd(a, b) * b; } ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) { ll t = n / k; if (n % k == 0) return t; return t + 1; } ll pw(ll a, ll b) { ll r = 1; while (b) { if (b & 1) r *= a; a *= a; b >>= 1; } return r; } ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) { ll r = 1 % mod; a %= mod; while (b) { if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod; a = a * a % mod; b >>= 1; } return r; } void solve() { ll n; cin >> n; vll h(n); for (ll i = 0; i < n; ++i) cin >> h[i]; vector a(n); for (ll i = 0; i < n; ++i) a[i] = {h[i], i + 1}; sort(all(a)); vll plan1(n); for (ll i = 0; i < n; ++i) plan1[i] = a[i].second; vll freq(2001, 0); for (ll x : h) freq[x]++; ll cnt2 = 0, cnt3 = 0; for (ll f : freq) { if (f >= 3) cnt3++; else if (f == 2) cnt2++; } if (cnt3 == 0 && cnt2 < 2) { cout << "NO\n"; return; } cout << "YES\n"; for (ll i = 0; i < n; ++i) cout << plan1[i] << " \n"[i == n - 1]; ll pos1 = -1, len1 = 0; for (ll i = 0; i < n; ) { ll j = i; while (j < n && a[j].first == a[i].first) j++; ll sz = j - i; if (sz >= 2) { pos1 = i; len1 = sz; break; } i = j; } vll plan2 = plan1; swap(plan2[pos1], plan2[pos1 + 1]); for (ll i = 0; i < n; ++i) cout << plan2[i] << " \n"[i == n - 1]; vll plan3; if (len1 >= 3) { plan3 = plan1; swap(plan3[pos1], plan3[pos1 + 2]); } else { ll pos2 = -1; for (ll i = pos1 + 2; i < n; ) { ll j = i; while (j < n && a[j].first == a[i].first) j++; ll sz = j - i; if (sz >= 2) { pos2 = i; break; } i = j; } plan3 = plan1; swap(plan3[pos2], plan3[pos2 + 1]); } for (ll i = 0; i < n; ++i) cout << plan3[i] << " \n"[i == n - 1]; } int main() { fastio; solve(); return 0; }
